questions.question: What is the purpose of the native VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk?
options: To carry untagged traffic across the trunk
To assign the highest priority to voice traffic
questions.explanation: The native VLAN carries untagged frames on an 802.1Q trunk. Both ends must agree on the native VLAN — a mismatch causes traffic leakage between VLANs, which is a security risk.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 17: Trunking; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 8

questions.question: Which STP port state forwards frames AND learns MAC addresses?
questions.explanation: Only the Forwarding state both forwards frames AND learns MAC addresses. Learning learns MACs but does not forward. Blocking does neither. Listening processes BPDUs only.
questions.source_ref: Wendell Odom - CCNA 200-301 OCG Vol 2, Chapter 9: STP; CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q66

questions.question: What are three valid reasons to create VLANs? (Choose three)
options: Moves, adds, and changes are simplified
Allows switches to route between VLANs natively
questions.explanation: VLANs improve MACs (Moves/Adds/Changes), reduce admin overhead, and limit broadcast domains. Switches do NOT route natively — inter-VLAN routing requires a router or Layer 3 switch.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q37; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16

questions.question: What is the purpose of Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)?
questions.explanation: STP (802.1D) prevents Layer 2 bridging loops by placing redundant ports in a blocking state. Cisco supports PVST+ which runs a separate STP instance per VLAN.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q66; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 9

questions.question: Which switching mode receives the complete frame before forwarding and performs a CRC check?
questions.explanation: Store-and-forward: receives the entire frame, runs CRC check, then forwards. Cut-through forwards after reading only the destination MAC — lower latency but passes corrupt frames. All modern switches use store-and-forward.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q38; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 5

questions.question: Which devices segment collision domains, and which segment broadcast domains?
options: Routers segment collision domains; switches segment broadcast domains
Switches segment collision domains; routers segment broadcast domains
Hubs segment collision domains; switches segment broadcast domains
Routers segment both collision and broadcast domains
questions.explanation: Switches create separate collision domains per port (each port = one collision domain). Routers create separate broadcast domains per interface (routers do not forward broadcasts). Hubs do not segment either.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q16-17; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 2

questions.question: What is the channel configuration of ISDN BRI?
questions.explanation: ISDN BRI (Basic Rate Interface) = 2B + 1D. B (Bearer) channels = 64 kbps each for voice/data. D (Delta) channel = 16 kbps for signaling/call setup. Total = 144 kbps. BRI is the residential variant.
questions.source_ref: Boson Software CCNA Topics Not in ICRC (1999); Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 15

questions.question: What is the channel configuration of ISDN PRI in the US and Japan?
questions.explanation: ISDN PRI (Primary Rate Interface) US/Japan = 23B + 1D channels on a T1 = 1.544 Mbps. Europe/World uses 30B + 1D on an E1 = 2.048 Mbps. PRI is the business/enterprise variant equivalent to a full T1/E1.
questions.source_ref: Boson Software CCNA Topics Not in ICRC (1999); Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 15

questions.question: In ISDN protocol series naming, what does the 'I' series represent?
options: ISDN integration with existing telephone networks
questions.explanation: ISDN protocol series: E = integration with existing telephone networks; Q = switching and signaling (e.g., Q.921 LAPD, Q.931); I = concepts, terminology, and services (e.g., I.430, I.431). These define the standards framework.
questions.source_ref: Boson Software CCNA Topics Not in ICRC (1999); Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 15

questions.question: Which switching method reads only the destination MAC address before forwarding — the fastest but may forward corrupt frames?
questions.explanation: Cut-through (fast-forward) starts forwarding after reading only the 6-byte destination MAC, minimizing latency. The drawback: no CRC check, so corrupt frames are forwarded. Fragment-free reads the first 64 bytes (collision fragment check only). Store-and-forward reads the whole frame and checks CRC.
questions.source_ref: Boson Software CCNA Topics Not in ICRC (1999); Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 5

questions.question: Store-and-forward switching performs a CRC check on every received frame and drops corrupt frames before forwarding.
questions.explanation: Store-and-forward buffers the entire frame, runs a Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC), and only forwards frames that pass. This eliminates error propagation at the cost of higher latency (latency is variable based on frame size). This is the default on modern Cisco Catalyst switches.
questions.source_ref: Boson Software CCNA Topics Not in ICRC (1999); Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 5

questions.question: Which command sequence correctly creates VLAN 10 named 'SALES' and assigns port Gi0/1 to it on a Cisco switch?
options: vlan 10 → name SALES → interface Gi0/1 → switchport mode access → switchport access vlan 10
vlan 10 SALES → interface Gi0/1 → access vlan 10
interface Gi0/1 → vlan 10 name SALES → mode access
create vlan 10 SALES → port Gi0/1 access vlan 10
questions.explanation: VLANs are created in global config or VLAN database: 'vlan 10' then 'name SALES'. Ports are assigned in interface config: 'switchport mode access' makes it an access port, then 'switchport access vlan 10' assigns it to VLAN 10. Verify with 'show vlan brief'.
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 22 VLAN Configuration; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 16

questions.question: What command configures a trunk link on a Cisco switch and permits VLANs 10 and 20?
options: switchport mode trunk → switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20
questions.explanation: 'switchport mode trunk' enables 802.1Q trunking. By default all VLANs are allowed. 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20' restricts to only VLANs 10 and 20. Use 'add' to append (e.g., 'switchport trunk allowed vlan add 30') rather than replacing the list.
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 22 VLAN Configuration; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 17

questions.question: Which inter-VLAN routing method uses a single router physical interface with sub-interfaces — one per VLAN?
options: Traditional inter-VLAN routing (separate physical interface per VLAN)
questions.explanation: Router-on-a-stick (ROAS) uses one physical trunk interface divided into logical sub-interfaces (e.g., Gi0/0.10, Gi0/0.20), each configured with 'encapsulation dot1q [vlan-id]' and an IP address. More scalable than separate physical interfaces, but the single link is a bottleneck.
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 22 Inter-VLAN Configuration; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 18

questions.question: Which command displays the currently active (running) configuration of a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: The 'show running-config' command displays the current configuration stored in RAM. 'show startup-config' shows what's in NVRAM (saved configuration). In IOS pre-10.3, the equivalent was 'write term'.

questions.question: What does the 'show startup-config' command display?
options: The IOS version and boot image
The saved configuration stored in NVRAM
questions.explanation: The 'show startup-config' command displays the configuration saved in NVRAM (non-volatile RAM). This is the config loaded at boot time. The pre-10.3 equivalent was 'show config'.
questions.source_ref: CCNA_CramGuide.htm section 20; CCNA_help.htm memory types table

questions.question: Which command saves the running configuration to NVRAM (makes it persistent across reboots)?
questions.explanation: 'copy running-config startup-config' copies the current RAM configuration to NVRAM so it persists after a reload. The old command (pre-IOS 10.3) was 'write memory' or 'wr mem'.

questions.question: Where is the Cisco IOS operating system image normally stored on a router?
questions.explanation: The Cisco IOS image is stored in Flash memory (EEPROM). Flash is non-volatile persistent storage. RAM holds the running configuration and routing tables. NVRAM holds the startup configuration. ROM holds the bootstrap and a minimal IOS for recovery.
questions.source_ref: Talbert talbert4.htm; CCNA_Cramsession.htm IOS/Routing; CCNA_help.htm memory types

questions.question: What IOS command is used to back up the IOS software image to a TFTP server?
questions.explanation: 'copy flash tftp' copies the IOS image FROM flash TO a TFTP server (backup). 'copy tftp flash' does the reverse (restore from TFTP to flash). 'copy running-config tftp' backs up the configuration, not the IOS image.
questions.source_ref: Talbert talbert4.htm; CCNA_Cramsession.htm copy flash tftp

questions.question: A router cannot write to flash memory because the IOS is running from flash. What is the recommended solution to allow flash updates?
options: Use 'copy tftp flash' directly and override the read-only lock
Boot the router from ROM, then update flash while IOS is not using it
Erase flash first, then reload from TFTP
Use 'write erase' to clear the configuration
questions.explanation: Some routers run IOS from flash, which puts flash in read-only mode. The trick is to boot from ROM (minimal IOS), which frees flash memory so it can be written. Then use 'copy tftp flash' to update the IOS image.

questions.question: What does the Router> prompt indicate?
questions.explanation: The Router> prompt indicates User EXEC mode — limited commands for checking status. To enter Privileged EXEC mode (Router#), type 'enable'. To return from privileged to user mode, type 'disable'.
questions.source_ref: CCNA_help.htm Router Modes table; CCNA_CramGuide.htm section 16

questions.question: Which command enters Global Configuration mode from Privileged EXEC mode?
questions.explanation: 'configure terminal' (shortened to 'conf t') enters Global Configuration mode from Privileged EXEC (Router#). The prompt changes to Router(config)#. Changes made here affect the running configuration.

questions.question: What is the IEEE standard number for Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)?
questions.explanation: STP (Spanning Tree Protocol) is defined by IEEE 802.1d. It prevents switching loops by placing redundant ports in blocking state. 802.1q is the VLAN tagging standard, 802.3 is Ethernet, 802.11 is wireless.

questions.question: What is microsegmentation in the context of LAN switching?
options: Each host connected directly to its own dedicated switch port, getting full bandwidth
Using cut-through switching to reduce latency
Connecting multiple switches together using trunk links
questions.explanation: Microsegmentation means each host is connected to its own dedicated port on the switch, effectively giving each host its own collision domain and the full amount of available bandwidth. This is a key advantage of LAN switches over shared hubs.

questions.question: Which switching method reads only the destination MAC address into memory before beginning to forward the frame?
questions.explanation: Cut-Through (Cisco calls this 'Fast Forward') reads only the destination MAC address before forwarding. It has the lowest latency but no error checking. Store-and-Forward reads the entire frame and performs CRC. Fragment-Free reads the first 64 bytes.

questions.question: Full-Duplex Ethernet requires which of the following?
options: A single workstation connected directly to a switch port, with a NIC supporting full-duplex
At least two workstations sharing a hub
A minimum cable length of 100 meters
questions.explanation: Full-duplex requires: (1) a single workstation on a dedicated switch port, (2) a NIC that supports full-duplex. With separate send and receive wires and no shared medium, collisions are eliminated. Half-duplex uses CSMA/CD and can use only ~50% of available bandwidth.

questions.question: A switch operating in Store-and-Forward mode receives a frame with a CRC error. What does it do?
options: Forwards the frame and logs the error
Forwards the frame to all ports
Sends an ICMP error back to the source
questions.explanation: Store-and-Forward switches read the entire frame into a buffer and perform a CRC (cyclic redundancy check). If the frame has errors, it is discarded. This is the main advantage over Cut-Through, which forwards corrupt frames. The Catalyst 5000 uses Store-and-Forward.

questions.question: At which OSI layer does Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) operate?
questions.explanation: CDP runs over the Data Link layer only — it is media- and protocol-independent. CDP runs on all Cisco equipment (routers, switches, access servers) and on all media supporting SNAP (LAN, Frame Relay, ATM). CDP is used to discover directly connected Cisco devices.

questions.question: Which command shows detailed information about directly connected Cisco neighbors including IP address, platform, and IOS version?
questions.explanation: 'show cdp neighbors detail' provides full details about each neighbor including IP address, IOS version, capabilities, and platform/model. 'show cdp neighbors' (without 'detail') shows a summary table with device ID, local interface, holdtime, capability, and platform.

questions.question: What is EtherChannel (also called Port Channel or LAG)?
options: A protocol that prevents switching loops
A technology that bundles multiple physical links between switches into one logical link
A method for creating VLANs on a switch
questions.explanation: EtherChannel bundles 2–8 physical Ethernet links into a single logical link, increasing bandwidth and providing redundancy. From STP's perspective, it appears as a single link (preventing STP from blocking redundant physical links). Negotiated via LACP (802.3ad) or PAgP (Cisco proprietary).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 23: EtherChannel; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: In Spanning Tree Protocol, what is a Bridge Protocol Data Unit (BPDU)?
options: A frame used to transport VoIP traffic
A special frame switches use to exchange STP information and elect the Root Bridge
A management packet used by CDP
questions.explanation: BPDUs are special frames that STP-enabled switches send to each other to share topology information. BPDUs are used to elect the Root Bridge (lowest Bridge ID wins), determine port roles (Root Port, Designated Port, Blocked Port), and detect topology changes. Sent every 2 seconds by default.
questions.source_ref: CCNA_Cramsession.htm STP section; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 20: STP

questions.question: In STP, how is the Root Bridge selected?
options: The switch with the highest Bridge ID (highest MAC address) becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC) becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the most ports becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the highest IP address becomes Root Bridge
questions.explanation: The Root Bridge is the switch with the LOWEST Bridge ID. Bridge ID = Bridge Priority (default 32768, configurable in multiples of 4096) + MAC address. To make a switch the Root Bridge, lower its priority using 'spanning-tree vlan {id} priority {value}' or 'spanning-tree vlan {id} root primary'.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 20: STP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the correct IOS command to set a DCE clock rate of 56 Kbps on a serial interface?
questions.explanation: 'clock rate 56000' (two words, in bps) is the correct syntax, applied under interface configuration mode (config-if). This command is only valid on DCE serial interfaces (the end of the cable connected to the CSU/DSU simulated device). DTE interfaces do not set clock rate.

questions.question: What is the correct IOS command to set the bandwidth of a serial interface to 56 Kbps?
questions.explanation: The 'bandwidth' command takes a value in kilobits (kbps). So for 56 Kbps, use 'bandwidth 56'. For T1 (1.544 Mbps), use 'bandwidth 1544'. This is a logical setting used by routing protocols (like OSPF) for metric calculation — it does NOT change the actual interface speed.

questions.question: Which of the following are benefits of segmenting a network with ROUTERS? Choose three.
options: Controls broadcasts (each router interface is its own broadcast domain)
questions.explanation: Routers provide: (1) increased per-node bandwidth by reducing collision domains, (2) broadcast control — routers do not forward broadcasts between interfaces, limiting broadcast storms, (3) reduced collisions — each router port is its own collision domain. Routers do NOT increase frame size.

questions.question: What does the 'show version' command display on a Cisco router?
options: System hardware, IOS software version, boot images, and configuration register
questions.explanation: 'show version' displays: router model/hardware, IOS version, boot image source (flash/TFTP/ROM), uptime, RAM/Flash/NVRAM sizes, and the configuration register value (e.g., 0x2102 = normal boot from flash). It does NOT show the running configuration — use 'show running-config' for that.

questions.question: Which IOS command verifies connectivity at the Network layer by using ICMP Echo/Reply messages?
questions.explanation: Ping uses ICMP Echo Request and Echo Reply to verify Layer 3 (Network) connectivity and logical address reachability. Telnet verifies application-layer connectivity (Layer 7). Traceroute uses TTL values to identify each hop along the path. 'show ip route' displays the routing table without sending any packets.

questions.question: What does the IOS context-sensitive help system display if you type 'show ?' at the router prompt?
options: An error — '?' is not a valid character
A list of all commands starting with 'show'
questions.explanation: Typing '?' after a partial command (like 'show ?') displays a list of all valid completions for that partial command. Typing '?' with no preceding text shows all available commands at that prompt level. Note: 'sh ar?' (no space) shows commands that COMPLETE 'ar', while 'sh ar ?' (space) shows arguments for the 'ar' command.

questions.question: A serial interface shows 'Serial0 is down, line protocol is up'. What does this mean?
options: The interface is disconnected but keepalives are being received
The interface works but keepalives are not received
The interface is in loopback mode
questions.explanation: The combination 'interface down, protocol up' is NOT possible. Valid states: (1) Up/Up = fully working, (2) Administratively Down/Down = 'shutdown' configured, (3) Down/Down = no carrier detect (physical problem, call the carrier), (4) Up/Down = interface OK but no keepalives (bad CSU/DSU), (5) Up/Down(looped) = CSU/DSU in loopback mode.

questions.question: When you enter a configuration command on a Cisco router, when does it take effect?
questions.explanation: Configuration commands on Cisco IOS take effect IMMEDIATELY when entered — they go straight into the running-config in RAM. However, they are not persistent until saved to NVRAM with 'copy running-config startup-config'. If the router reboots without saving, the changes are lost.

questions.question: Which of the following does 'show cdp neighbors detail' reveal? (Choose all correct answers represented as one option)
options: Router platform, IOS version, all Layer 3 addresses, incoming/outgoing port, and router name
Only the IP address of the neighbor
SNMP community strings and VTP domain
questions.explanation: The 'show cdp neighbors detail' command reveals: Device ID (hostname), all Layer 3 addresses (one per protocol), platform/model, IOS version, local/remote port, and holdtime. It does NOT show subnet masks or SNMP strings.

questions.question: What is HDLC an abbreviation for?
questions.explanation: HDLC (High-level Data Link Control) is a bit-oriented, synchronous data link layer protocol developed by ISO, derived from IBM's SDLC. It is the default encapsulation on Cisco serial interfaces. Cisco's version is proprietary and adds a type field not in the ISO standard.

questions.question: What is the Cisco IOS keyboard shortcut to recall the NEXT command in command history?
questions.explanation: Ctrl-N (or Down Arrow) recalls the next command in the history buffer, moving forward in history. Ctrl-P (or Up Arrow) recalls the previous command. Ctrl-Z exits to privileged EXEC. Ctrl-C aborts the current command.

questions.question: A 'show interfaces serial0' output shows 'Serial0 is up, line protocol is down'. What does this most likely indicate?
options: No keepalives are being received (clocking/layer 2 issue)
The interface is administratively shut down
questions.explanation: Interface up / protocol down means Layer 1 is working (carrier detected) but Layer 2 has failed. Most commonly this means keepalives are not being received, which indicates a clocking issue, encapsulation mismatch, or the remote end is not responding. 'Administratively down' shows a different message.

questions.question: Given subnets 128.252.4.0, 128.252.8.0, 128.252.12.0, 128.252.16.0 — what subnet mask is in use?
questions.explanation: The subnets increment by 4 in the third octet (4, 8, 12, 16...). An increment of 4 means the block size is 4, which corresponds to borrowing 6 bits in the third octet: 255.255.252.0 (/22). 256 - 252 = 4 confirms the block size.

questions.question: A router interface is described as 10.10.1.1/23. What is its subnet mask in dotted-decimal?
questions.explanation: /23 means 23 bits of network mask. First two octets = 16 bits (255.255). Remaining 7 bits in third octet = 11111110 = 254. So /23 = 255.255.254.0. A /23 covers two Class C blocks (e.g., 10.10.0.0 - 10.10.1.255).

questions.question: What does the wildcard mask 0.0.7.255 specify in an ACL statement?
options: Match any address in the range .0 to .255 of the network
Match 8 contiguous subnets (the range 10.1.8.0 through 10.1.15.0 when combined with 10.1.8.0)
questions.explanation: Wildcard mask 0.0.7.255 means: octets 1,2 must match exactly; octet 3 can vary by up to 7 (binary: 00000111); octet 4 can be anything (255). So `10.1.8.0 0.0.7.255` matches addresses from 10.1.8.0 through 10.1.15.255 — a range of 8 networks.

questions.question: What command verifies the configured access lists on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `show access-lists` displays all configured access lists (IP, IPX, etc.) with match counts per line. `show ip access-lists` shows only IP access lists. `show ip interface <if>` shows which ACL is applied inbound/outbound on an interface. Both `show access-lists` and `show ip interface` are commonly used to verify ACL operation.

questions.question: What command displays all PVCs configured on a Frame Relay interface?
questions.explanation: `show frame-relay pvc` displays all PVC (Permanent Virtual Circuit) statistics including DLCI numbers, PVC status (Active/Inactive/Deleted), input/output packet counts, BECN/FECN/DE counts. `show frame-relay map` shows static and dynamic DLCI-to-IP address mappings. `show frame-relay lmi` shows LMI statistics.

questions.question: What command configures a Message of the Day (MOTD) banner on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `banner motd <delimiter><message><delimiter>` sets the MOTD banner shown to all users before login. The delimiter (e.g., #) marks the start and end of the message. Example: `banner motd # Authorized Access Only #`. Other banner types: `banner login` (shown at login prompt), `banner exec` (shown after login). MOTD is shown first.

questions.question: What are the five STP port states in order from blocked to forwarding?
questions.explanation: 802.1d STP port states: Blocking (receives BPDUs, no data forwarding, 20 sec max). Listening (sends/receives BPDUs, elects root/designated ports, 15 sec). Learning (learns MACs but no data forwarding, 15 sec). Forwarding (normal operation). Disabled (manually shut down). Total convergence time: up to 50 seconds! RSTP (802.1w) reduces this dramatically.

questions.question: What is Rapid PVST+ and how does it improve on 802.1d STP?
options: It removes STP entirely, relying on ECMP for loop prevention
It is Cisco's implementation of 802.1w (RSTP) per-VLAN, converging in seconds instead of 30-50 seconds
It uses hardware ASICs instead of software for STP processing
It runs on Layer 3 instead of Layer 2
questions.explanation: Rapid PVST+ = Cisco's per-VLAN implementation of 802.1w (RSTP). Key improvements: port roles added (Alternate, Backup), faster state transitions (discarding → learning → forwarding), proposal/agreement mechanism for rapid transitions, edge ports (PortFast equivalent) go directly to forwarding. Convergence in <1 second vs 30-50 seconds for 802.1d. Default on modern Cisco switches.

questions.question: What is the difference between an access port and a trunk port on a Cisco switch?
options: Access=carries multiple VLANs tagged with 802.1Q; Trunk=carries only one VLAN
Access=carries traffic for one VLAN (untagged); Trunk=carries traffic for multiple VLANs (802.1Q tagged)
Access=Layer 3 routed port; Trunk=Layer 2 switched port
Access=uplink to router; Trunk=connection to end devices
questions.explanation: Access port: connects to end devices (PCs, phones), carries traffic for ONE VLAN, frames are untagged. Configured with: `switchport mode access` + `switchport access vlan <id>`. Trunk port: connects to other switches or routers, carries traffic for MULTIPLE VLANs using 802.1Q tagging, each frame tagged with VLAN ID. Configured with: `switchport mode trunk`.

questions.question: What is the native VLAN on a trunk port and what security concern does it present?
options: The native VLAN carries management traffic only; no security concern
The native VLAN (default VLAN 1) carries untagged traffic on trunk ports; can be exploited by VLAN hopping (double-tagging)
The native VLAN is always VLAN 99; it carries spanning tree traffic
The native VLAN must match the management VLAN to work correctly
questions.explanation: The native VLAN is the VLAN that carries untagged frames on a trunk (default VLAN 1). Security concern: double-tagging attack — attacker sends a frame tagged with native VLAN ID; switch strips native tag, inner tag remains, frame forwarded to target VLAN. Mitigation: change native VLAN from default: `switchport trunk native vlan 999` (use an unused VLAN). Also tag native VLAN explicitly: `vlan dot1q tag native`.

questions.question: In Cisco IOS, what does the caret symbol (^) in a failed command error message indicate?
options: Numeric data is required at this position
The point where an incorrect command, keyword, or argument was entered
The point where you press Return to complete the command
The beginning of a required value
questions.explanation: When IOS encounters an invalid command, it displays '% Invalid input detected at '^' marker.' The ^ (caret) points to the exact character where IOS could not parse the input. This helps quickly identify typos. Example: `R2#sh clack` → `^ % Invalid input detected` — the caret is under 'c' in 'clack'.

questions.question: Which subnet mask has the nickname 'serial mask' and why?
options: 255.255.255.252 — provides exactly 2 hosts, perfect for point-to-point serial links
255.255.255.240 — provides 14 hosts for medium WAN segments
255.255.255.192 — provides 62 hosts for large WAN segments
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.252 (/30) is called the 'serial mask' because a point-to-point WAN link needs exactly 2 hosts (one on each end). /30 provides 2^2 - 2 = 2 usable addresses. This minimizes IP address waste on WAN links. A /31 can also be used on point-to-point links (RFC 3021) — provides 2 addresses without subtraction (no network/broadcast).

questions.question: Which THREE of the following are valid reasons for creating VLANs?
options: Moves/Adds/Changes are simpler, limits broadcast domains, and the switch routes between VLANs
Moves/Adds/Changes are simpler, less administrative overhead, and limits broadcast domains
The switch routes between VLANs, limits collision domains, and reduces cabling
Limits broadcast domains, provides encryption, and enables Layer 3 switching
questions.explanation: VLANs provide: (1) Simplified Moves/Adds/Changes — a move within the same VLAN needs no IP reconfiguration. (2) Less administrative overhead — logical grouping independent of physical location. (3) Limited broadcast domains — each VLAN is its own broadcast domain, reducing unnecessary traffic. Note: switches do NOT route between VLANs by default — that requires a router (ROAS) or Layer 3 switch.

questions.question: What is CAPWAP and what role does it play in wireless networking?
options: A wireless encryption protocol that replaces WPA2
A tunneling protocol (UDP 5246/5247) that allows a WLC to centrally manage lightweight APs, carrying 802.11 data and control traffic
A Cisco-proprietary routing protocol for wireless mesh networks
A QoS protocol for prioritizing wireless VoIP traffic
questions.explanation: CAPWAP (Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points, RFC 5415) is the standard protocol used between a WLC (Wireless LAN Controller) and lightweight APs. It creates two UDP tunnels: Control (UDP 5246) for management/configuration, Data (UDP 5247) for 802.11 user traffic. All traffic goes through the WLC, enabling centralized policy, roaming, and management. APs in lightweight mode are called LAPs.

questions.question: What are the non-overlapping Wi-Fi channels for 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands?
options: Both bands have channels 1-13 with channels 1, 7, 13 non-overlapping
questions.explanation: 2.4 GHz band: channels 1-14 but only channels 1, 6, 11 are non-overlapping (each 22 MHz wide) in North America — essential to minimize co-channel interference. 5 GHz band: 24+ channels available (36, 40, 44, 48, 52, etc.), all non-overlapping because they're 20 MHz wide and spaced 5 MHz apart. 5 GHz has higher bandwidth but shorter range than 2.4 GHz.

questions.question: What is the difference between LACP and PAgP for EtherChannel negotiation?
options: LACP is Cisco proprietary; PAgP is IEEE 802.3ad standard
LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) is an open standard; PAgP is Cisco proprietary. Both negotiate EtherChannel formation between switches
LACP uses TCP; PAgP uses UDP for negotiation
LACP is for Layer 3; PAgP is for Layer 2 EtherChannel
questions.explanation: EtherChannel negotiation protocols: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad/802.1AX) — open standard, works between any vendor. Modes: Active (initiates), Passive (responds). PAgP (Port Aggregation Protocol) — Cisco proprietary, Cisco-to-Cisco only. Modes: Desirable (initiates), Auto (responds). Both require matching speed/duplex/VLAN settings. Static 'on' mode creates EtherChannel without negotiation.

questions.question: What is STP PortFast and when should it be used?
options: A feature that speeds up STP on trunk ports between switches
A feature that transitions access ports directly to forwarding state (bypassing listening/learning), used on ports connected only to end devices
A feature that makes switches elect a new Root Bridge faster
A feature that enables rapid renegotiation of trunk links
questions.explanation: PortFast skips the STP Listening (15s) and Learning (15s) states, allowing a port to go directly to Forwarding. This prevents the 30-second delay that would cause DHCP timeouts or login failures on end devices. IMPORTANT: Use ONLY on access ports connected to end devices, NEVER on switch-to-switch links (would cause topology changes). Configured with: `spanning-tree portfast` on the interface, or `spanning-tree portfast default` globally.

questions.question: What is BPDU Guard and what does it do when a BPDU is received?
options: Prevents BPDUs from being sent; shuts down the switch if BPDUs exceed threshold
Protects PortFast ports — if a BPDU is received (indicating a switch connected), the port is immediately err-disabled
A feature that encrypts BPDUs for security
Blocks all BPDUs from non-root switches
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard protects PortFast ports from rogue switches. If a BPDU is received on a PortFast-enabled port, BPDU Guard immediately puts the port in err-disabled state — preventing that switch from participating in STP and potentially becoming Root Bridge. Configured with: `spanning-tree bpduguard enable` per-port, or `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` globally. Re-enable with shutdown/no shutdown.

questions.question: Which Cisco router memory type stores the startup configuration file?
questions.explanation: NVRAM (Non-Volatile RAM) stores the startup-config. RAM holds the running-config and loaded IOS. Flash stores the IOS image. ROM holds bootstrap/POST and ROM monitor.

questions.question: Which router memory type stores the currently running IOS image and the running configuration?
questions.explanation: RAM (main memory) holds the running IOS image, running-config, routing tables, and ARP cache. Contents are lost on power-off. Flash holds the stored IOS image. NVRAM holds startup-config.

questions.question: What is stored in Flash memory on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: Flash memory stores the Cisco IOS software image(s). It is persistent (retains content without power) and typically uses PCMCIA cards or SIMMs. NVRAM stores startup-config; RAM stores the running IOS and running-config.

questions.question: What is the correct default Cisco IOS boot sequence for loading the IOS image?
questions.explanation: By default, the router first looks in Flash for an IOS image, then tries a TFTP server (if configured), and finally falls back to ROM (ROM monitor). The `boot system` commands in startup-config can change this order.

questions.question: Which command saves the running configuration to NVRAM?
questions.explanation: `copy running-config startup-config` (or older `write memory`) saves the current running config from RAM to NVRAM, making it the new startup-config. The reverse (`copy startup-config running-config`) merges startup-config into RAM.

questions.question: What is the difference between `enable password` and `enable secret` on a Cisco router?
options: They are identical; `enable secret` is just newer syntax
`enable secret` uses MD5 encryption; `enable password` is stored in cleartext by default
`enable password` uses MD5 encryption; `enable secret` is stored in cleartext
`enable secret` applies only to console access
questions.explanation: `enable secret` uses a Cisco-proprietary one-way MD5 hash and is always encrypted in the config. `enable password` is stored in cleartext unless `service password-encryption` is configured. When both are set, `enable secret` takes precedence.

questions.question: What metric does RIP use to determine the best path?
questions.explanation: RIP uses hop count as its only metric. A hop count of 15 is the maximum; 16 is considered unreachable (infinity). This simple metric makes RIP unsuitable for large networks but easy to configure.

questions.question: How often does RIP broadcast its full routing table updates by default?
questions.explanation: RIP broadcasts its complete routing table every 30 seconds by default. This periodic flooding is a characteristic of distance-vector protocols. IGRP updates every 90 seconds; OSPF and EIGRP use event-triggered updates.

questions.question: What is the purpose of split horizon in distance-vector routing?
options: It divides large networks into smaller segments
It prevents routing information from being sent back out the interface it was received on
It assigns a maximum hop count of 15 to prevent loops
It sends poison reverse updates to all neighbors
questions.explanation: Split horizon prevents a router from advertising a route back through the same interface it learned the route from. This prevents two-node routing loops. The rule: 'it is never useful to send information about a route back in the direction from which it came.'

questions.question: What is the purpose of hold-down timers in RIP?
options: To delay routing updates for 30 seconds on startup
To prevent a route from being reinstated prematurely after it has gone down
To hold routing table entries for 90 seconds before deletion
To time the interval between full routing table broadcasts
questions.explanation: Hold-down timers prevent incorrect routing updates from reinstating a downed route. When a route goes bad, the router holds down changes to that route for a period slightly longer than the time needed to propagate the failure throughout the network. This prevents the count-to-infinity problem.

questions.question: Which algorithm do link-state routing protocols such as OSPF use to calculate the shortest path?
questions.explanation: Link-state protocols like OSPF use Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm to calculate the best loop-free path. Distance-vector protocols like RIP and IGRP use the Bellman-Ford algorithm. EIGRP uses DUAL (Diffusing Update ALGorithm).

questions.question: Which tool verifies application-layer connectivity between source and destination?
questions.explanation: Telnet verifies application-layer (Layer 7) connectivity because it uses the full TCP/IP stack. Ping uses ICMP to verify Layer 3 (network layer) connectivity. Traceroute uses TTL expiry to identify routers along the path.

questions.question: Which routing source has the lowest (most trusted) administrative distance?
questions.explanation: Administrative Distance (AD) indicates trustworthiness: lower = more trusted. Directly connected = 0, Static = 1, EIGRP summary = 5, BGP external = 20, EIGRP internal = 90, IGRP = 100, OSPF = 110, RIP = 120. A directly connected network is the most trusted. A higher AD means the router prefers routes from other sources.

questions.question: What is the OSPF cost formula and what is the default reference bandwidth?
questions.explanation: OSPF cost = Reference Bandwidth / Interface Bandwidth. Default reference bandwidth is 100 Mbps (100,000,000 bps). FastEthernet (100 Mbps) = cost 1, Ethernet (10 Mbps) = cost 10. Problem: GigE and faster all get cost 1 by default. Use `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 1000` to differentiate GigE links (cost 1) from FastEthernet (cost 10).

questions.question: What metric components does EIGRP use by default?
options: Bandwidth, delay, load, reliability, and MTU
questions.explanation: EIGRP uses bandwidth and delay by default in its composite metric formula. Load, reliability, and MTU are also available (K-values) but disabled by default (K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0). EIGRP's metric uses: Metric = [K1×BW + (K2×BW)/(256-load) + K3×Delay] × [K5/(reliability+K4)].

questions.question: What is the OSPF Router ID and how is it selected?
options: The IP address of the highest-numbered interface
Manually configured, or highest loopback IP, or highest active interface IP
The IP address of the management interface
questions.explanation: OSPF Router ID selection priority: 1) Manually configured (`router-id` command), 2) Highest IP on a loopback interface, 3) Highest IP on any active interface at OSPF startup. The Router ID uniquely identifies the OSPF router in the LSDB. Loopback interfaces are preferred because they never go down.

questions.question: What is the role of the Designated Router (DR) in OSPF?
options: The DR is the router with the highest priority that becomes the active router
The DR reduces LSA flooding by acting as a central point for OSPF adjacencies on multi-access networks
The DR is responsible for propagating default routes
The DR processes all inter-area routing
questions.explanation: On multi-access networks (Ethernet), OSPF elects a DR and BDR to reduce the number of OSPF adjacencies and LSA flooding. All routers form adjacency with the DR and BDR. The DR collects and distributes LSAs. Without DR/BDR, n routers would need n(n-1)/2 adjacencies. DR election: highest priority (default 1), then highest Router ID.

questions.question: What command configures a default route pointing to next-hop 203.0.113.1?
questions.explanation: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>` configures a default route (gateway of last resort). 0.0.0.0/0 matches all destinations. `ip default-gateway` is used on switches (not routers) when IP routing is disabled. The default route is used when no more specific route exists in the routing table.

questions.question: What summary route covers 192.168.0.0/24 through 192.168.3.0/24?
questions.explanation: 4 networks: 192.168.0.0, 192.168.1.0, 192.168.2.0, 192.168.3.0. In binary, these differ in the last 2 bits of the third octet (00, 01, 10, 11). The common prefix is the first 22 bits: 192.168.00xxxxxx. Summary = 192.168.0.0/22. This covers .0.0 to .3.255. /21 covers 8 networks (too broad).

questions.question: When multiple routes to the same destination exist, which one does a router prefer?
options: The route with the highest administrative distance
The route with the lowest administrative distance; if tied, lowest metric
The route with the most specific prefix (longest match)
The most specific prefix first, then lowest AD, then lowest metric
questions.explanation: Router route selection: 1) Longest prefix match (most specific route wins), 2) Among routes with same prefix length, lowest Administrative Distance wins, 3) Among routes with same AD, lowest metric wins. Example: a /28 route beats a /24 even if the /24 has lower AD, because it's more specific.

questions.question: Which command displays the IP routing table on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `show ip route` displays the IP routing table, including connected (C), static (S), RIP (R), OSPF (O), EIGRP (D), BGP (B) routes along with their AD, metric, next-hop, and outgoing interface. `show ip protocols` shows which routing protocols are running. `show arp` shows the ARP table.

questions.question: Which command shows all interfaces and their current status (up/down) and IP addresses?
questions.explanation: `show ip interface brief` provides a concise table of all interfaces with: interface name, IP address, OK? status, Method, Status (physical), and Protocol (Layer 2). It's the quickest way to verify interface status. `show interfaces` shows detailed statistics. `show ip interface` shows ACL/IP configuration per interface.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 EUI-64 address format used for?
options: Generating a /64 network prefix from a MAC address
Creating a 64-bit interface identifier from a 48-bit MAC address for SLAAC
Compressing IPv6 addresses with leading zeros
questions.explanation: EUI-64 creates a 64-bit interface ID from a 48-bit MAC address: split MAC in half, insert FFFE in the middle, and flip the 7th bit (universal/local bit). Example: MAC 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E → EUI-64: 021A:2BFF:FE3C:4D5E. Used in SLAAC (StateLess Address AutoConfiguration) to auto-generate the host portion of an IPv6 address.

questions.question: What IPv6 mechanism replaces ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)?
options: Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP) using ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation/Advertisement
questions.explanation: IPv6 replaces ARP with NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) which uses ICMPv6 messages: Neighbor Solicitation (NS, type 135) and Neighbor Advertisement (NA, type 136) to resolve MAC addresses from IPv6 addresses. NDP also uses Router Solicitation (RS) and Router Advertisement (RA) for router/prefix discovery. NDP uses multicast instead of broadcast.

questions.question: What does the `network` command do in OSPF configuration?
options: Advertises a specific network to BGP peers
Activates OSPF on interfaces whose IP addresses match the network/wildcard, and advertises those networks
Creates a static route to the specified network
Sets the OSPF cost for the specified network
questions.explanation: `router ospf <process-id>` followed by `network <ip> <wildcard> area <area>` enables OSPF on matching interfaces and advertises those networks. Wildcard 0.0.0.0 matches a specific host IP; 0.0.0.255 matches a /24. Alternative: configure OSPF directly on interfaces with `ip ospf <process> area <area>` (modern approach).

questions.question: What is the EIGRP Feasible Successor?
options: The primary best-path route to a destination
A backup route that meets the feasibility condition (Reported Distance < Feasible Distance of successor)
The next router in the EIGRP topology table
A route that has been advertised but not yet installed in the routing table
questions.explanation: EIGRP terms: Successor = best route (lowest Feasible Distance/FD) installed in routing table. Feasible Successor = backup route in topology table, ready for instant failover, whose Reported Distance (RD) is less than the current FD. If the Successor fails, the Feasible Successor is promoted without running DUAL again, enabling sub-second convergence.

questions.question: What is the difference between an IGP (Interior Gateway Protocol) and an EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol)?
options: IGPs are link-state; EGPs are distance-vector
IGPs route within an autonomous system; EGPs route between autonomous systems
IGPs are open standards; EGPs are proprietary
IGPs use hop count; EGPs use bandwidth
questions.explanation: IGP (Interior Gateway Protocol) routes traffic within a single autonomous system (AS): RIP, EIGRP, OSPF, IS-IS. EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol) routes between autonomous systems: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the only current EGP. Internet service providers and large enterprises use BGP to exchange routing information between organizations.

questions.question: Which routing protocol is Cisco proprietary?
questions.explanation: EIGRP (Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol) was originally Cisco proprietary, though Cisco published an informational RFC (7868) in 2016. OSPF and IS-IS are open standards (IETF). RIPv2 is also an open standard. EIGRP is often compared to OSPF for enterprise networks — EIGRP is easier on Cisco-only networks; OSPF is required for multi-vendor.

questions.question: What is the correct sequence to enter interface configuration mode for GigabitEthernet 0/0?
options: Router# configure terminal → Router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet 0/0
questions.explanation: IOS mode hierarchy: User EXEC (Router>) → Privileged EXEC (Router#, via `enable`) → Global Config (Router(config)#, via `configure terminal`) → Interface Config (Router(config-if)#, via `interface <type> <number>`). Most configuration commands require global config or interface config mode.

questions.question: What information does `show version` provide?
options: Current interface IP addresses and status
IOS version, hardware model, uptime, memory, and config register
Active routing protocols and their neighbors
VLAN database and trunk port information
questions.explanation: `show version` displays: IOS software version and filename, hardware model and serial number, RAM/NVRAM/Flash memory sizes, uptime, config register value (important for boot behavior), and number/type of interfaces. Also shows the number of reloads. Useful for licensing, upgrade planning, and troubleshooting boot issues.

questions.question: Why might a serial interface show 'Serial0/0 is up, line protocol is down'?
options: The interface is administratively shut down
There is a Layer 2 issue: keepalive failure, encapsulation mismatch, or missing clock rate
The remote router is not configured
There is no IP address on the interface
questions.explanation: Interface status: 'up/up' = working. 'up/down' = Physical layer OK but Layer 2 problem: keepalive mismatch, encapsulation mismatch (one end HDLC, other PPP), missing `clock rate` on DCE side. 'down/down' = Physical problem (cable, hardware). 'administratively down/down' = `shutdown` command applied. Verify with `show interfaces serial 0/0`.

questions.question: Which command backs up the current IOS image from flash to a TFTP server?
questions.explanation: `copy flash tftp` copies the IOS image FROM flash TO the TFTP server (backup). `copy tftp flash` copies FROM the TFTP server TO flash (restore/upgrade). The syntax follows: `copy <source> <destination>`. Similarly, `copy running-config startup-config` saves RAM to NVRAM, and `copy running-config tftp` backs up the config.

questions.question: When would you boot a Cisco router from ROM instead of Flash?
options: When you want the fastest boot time
When Flash is in read-only mode (IOS loaded from it), allowing you to update Flash
When the router has no NVRAM
ROM booting is the default on all Cisco routers
questions.explanation: Some routers don't fully load IOS into RAM — portions remain in Flash (XIP mode), keeping Flash in read-only mode. To update the Flash IOS image, you must first boot from ROM (using `boot system rom`) so Flash becomes read/write. Then you can `copy tftp flash` to update it. This is important for IOS upgrade procedures.

questions.question: In OSPF multi-area design, what is the role of Area 0?
options: Area 0 is reserved for stub networks only
Area 0 is the backbone area; all other areas must connect to it
Area 0 is optional; non-zero areas can connect directly
Area 0 is the area with the highest security classification
questions.explanation: In OSPF, Area 0 (the backbone area) is mandatory for multi-area designs. All other OSPF areas must connect to Area 0 either physically or via a virtual link. ABRs (Area Border Routers) connect non-backbone areas to Area 0. This hierarchical design reduces LSA flooding and routing table sizes. Single-area OSPF still uses Area 0.

questions.question: A router has a route to 10.1.0.0/24 via OSPF and a route to 10.1.0.0/24 via RIP. Which route is installed in the routing table?
options: OSPF route (AD 110 vs RIP AD 120 — lower AD wins)
RIP route (RIP is preferred for IP routing)
questions.explanation: When two routing protocols advertise the same prefix, the route with the lowest Administrative Distance wins. OSPF AD = 110, RIP AD = 120. Lower AD = more trusted. So the OSPF route is installed. If ADs were equal, the lowest metric would win. Only one route per prefix is typically in the routing table (unless equal-cost load balancing).

questions.question: Which command checks the OSPF neighbor state on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `show ip ospf neighbor` shows OSPF neighbor table with: Neighbor ID, Priority, State (should be Full for point-to-point or Full/2-Way on multi-access), Dead Time, address, and interface. Full state = fully adjacent, LSAs exchanged. 2-Way = bidirectional hello exchange (DR/BDR relationship not established). Issues: stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE often means MTU mismatch.

questions.question: How does a static route appear in the routing table with `show ip route`?
options: S (static), with administrative distance 1 and metric 0
questions.explanation: Route codes in `show ip route`: C=Connected (AD 0), L=Local, S=Static (AD 1), R=RIP (AD 120), O=OSPF (AD 110), D=EIGRP (AD 90), B=BGP, IA=OSPF inter-area. Static routes: `S 10.0.0.0/8 [1/0] via 192.168.1.1` — [AD/metric]. Default route shows as `S* 0.0.0.0/0` (asterisk = candidate default route).

questions.question: What does `show cdp neighbors` display that `show cdp neighbors detail` does NOT?
options: `show cdp neighbors` shows the full IP address list; detail shows only one
`show cdp neighbors` shows a compact summary; detail shows IOS version, full IP addresses, and platform model
`show cdp neighbors` shows Layer 2 info; detail shows Layer 3 info only
questions.explanation: `show cdp neighbors` shows: Device ID, local interface, holdtime, capability, platform (abbreviated), and remote port. `show cdp neighbors detail` adds: full platform model, IOS version, all Layer 3 addresses (per protocol), VTP domain, native VLAN, and duplex. The `detail` keyword is needed for IP addresses and IOS version.

questions.question: Which parameters MUST match for two OSPF routers to form adjacency?
options: Area ID, hello/dead intervals, MTU, subnet mask, stub area flag, and authentication
questions.explanation: OSPF adjacency requirements: same Area ID, matching hello/dead intervals (default 10s/40s on broadcast, 30s/120s on NBMA), same subnet/mask on the link, matching stub area flags, same authentication key. MTU mismatch can cause EXSTART/EXCHANGE stuck state (use `ip ospf mtu-ignore`). Router ID and priority don't need to match.

questions.question: What command enables EIGRP for AS 100 and includes network 192.168.1.0/24?
options: Both A and B are correct
questions.explanation: Both work: `network 192.168.1.0` (classful — assumes /24 for Class C) and `network 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255` (classless with wildcard mask) both enable EIGRP on interfaces in that subnet. The wildcard mask gives more precision. EIGRP AS numbers (1-65535) must match between neighbors. `eigrp router-id` can set a static router ID similar to OSPF.

questions.question: What command enables IPv6 routing on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `ipv6 unicast-routing` (global config) enables IPv6 forwarding and routing on the router. Without it, the router only generates link-local addresses but won't forward IPv6 packets between interfaces. Also configure `ipv6 address <prefix>/<length>` on each interface. IPv6 static routes: `ipv6 route <prefix/length> <next-hop>`.

questions.question: What is OSPFv3?
options: OSPF version 3 adds security features for IPv4 routing
OSPFv3 is the version of OSPF for IPv6 routing (and modern versions support address families for both IPv4 and IPv6)
OSPFv3 replaces EIGRP for dual-stack networks
OSPFv3 is a Cisco proprietary extension of standard OSPF
questions.explanation: OSPFv3 (RFC 5340) was originally designed for IPv6 routing, using IPv6 link-local addresses for neighbor discovery. Modern Cisco implementations support 'address families' — OSPFv3 can run both IPv4 and IPv6 routing. OSPFv2 is IPv4-only. Key change: OSPFv3 uses router ID (IPv4-format 32-bit number) even for IPv6-only deployments.

questions.question: On an OSPF point-to-point network type, is a DR/BDR elected?
options: Yes, always — DR/BDR election is mandatory for all OSPF network types
No — DR/BDR is only elected on broadcast (multi-access) networks like Ethernet
Only if the network has more than 2 routers
The DR is elected but not the BDR on point-to-point
questions.explanation: DR/BDR election occurs only on broadcast multi-access networks (Ethernet) to reduce adjacency count. Point-to-point networks (serial, PPP) form a single full adjacency between exactly two routers — no need for DR/BDR. NBMA (Frame Relay) requires manual configuration of DR/BDR or use of point-to-point subinterfaces. Loopback interfaces use Host network type by default.

questions.question: What is BGP and when would a network engineer need to configure it?
options: Border Gateway Protocol — used for routing within a large enterprise campus network
Border Gateway Protocol — used for routing between autonomous systems, typically when connecting to multiple ISPs
BGP is Cisco proprietary and used with EIGRP for MPLS networks
BGP is used when OSPF reaches its maximum hop count
questions.explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol, RFC 4271) is the routing protocol of the Internet. It routes between Autonomous Systems (ASes). Network engineers need BGP when: connecting to multiple ISPs (multihoming), implementing traffic engineering with provider, or working at ISP level. eBGP = between different ASes; iBGP = within same AS. BGP uses TCP port 179.

questions.question: Which command shows the current configuration register value on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `show version` shows the current and the 'next boot' configuration register value at the bottom of the output (e.g., 'Configuration register is 0x2102'). The config register controls boot behavior: 0x2102 = normal boot (load IOS from Flash, then NVRAM config). 0x2142 = ignore NVRAM config on boot (password recovery). 0x2100 = boot to ROM monitor.

questions.question: In Cisco IOS password recovery, why is the configuration register changed to 0x2142?
options: To boot the router from TFTP instead of Flash
To bypass loading the startup configuration (which contains the forgotten password)
To enable ROM monitor mode for password reset
To increase the boot priority of Flash over NVRAM
questions.explanation: Password recovery procedure: 1) Interrupt boot (break key), 2) Change config register to 0x2142 in ROM monitor (rommon> `confreg 0x2142`), 3) Reload, 4) Router boots without loading startup-config (no passwords enforced), 5) `copy startup-config running-config` to see old config, 6) Set new password, 7) Change register back to 0x2102, 8) Save and reload.

questions.question: What is the effect of the `no shutdown` command on a router interface?
options: Removes the shutdown timer from the interface
Administratively enables the interface (brings it up from admin-down state)
Removes the IP address from the interface
questions.explanation: By default, router interfaces are administratively down — they show as 'Serial/GigabitEthernet is administratively down, line protocol is down.' The `no shutdown` command removes this admin-down state and enables the interface. Switch ports default to no shutdown. After `no shutdown`, the interface needs physical connectivity and correct configuration to reach up/up state.

questions.question: What does `traceroute` (or `tracert` on Windows) show?
options: Only the final destination IP and response time
Each hop along the path with IP address and round-trip time
The routing table entries on each hop
questions.explanation: Traceroute sends packets with incrementing TTL values (1, 2, 3...). Each router decrements TTL to 0 and sends back ICMP Time Exceeded, revealing its IP. This shows every Layer 3 hop. Useful for identifying where connectivity fails or where latency increases. `traceroute` (Cisco/Linux), `tracert` (Windows). Extended traceroute on Cisco allows custom source IP and probes.

questions.question: What is the default OSPF hello interval and dead interval on an Ethernet (broadcast) interface?
questions.explanation: OSPF timers on broadcast (Ethernet): Hello = 10 seconds, Dead = 40 seconds (4× hello). On NBMA (non-broadcast multi-access, e.g., Frame Relay): Hello = 30s, Dead = 120s. Timer mismatch prevents adjacency. Change with: `ip ospf hello-interval <sec>` (automatically sets dead interval to 4× hello). Rapid convergence: reduce hello to 1s, dead to 3s.

questions.question: What is the default EIGRP hello interval and hold time on a LAN interface?
questions.explanation: EIGRP defaults: LAN interfaces = Hello 5s, Hold 15s. Slow links (T1 or slower) = Hello 60s, Hold 180s. Hold time = how long to wait without a hello before declaring neighbor down (3× hello). Unlike OSPF, EIGRP timers don't need to match between neighbors. Configure: `ip hello-interval eigrp <as> <sec>` and `ip hold-time eigrp <as> <sec>`.

questions.question: What is an OSPF Type 1 LSA?
options: A summary LSA generated by ABRs for inter-area routes
A Router LSA generated by every router describing its directly connected links
An AS External LSA for routes redistributed from other protocols
A Network LSA generated by the DR on broadcast networks
questions.explanation: OSPF LSA types: Type 1 (Router LSA) = generated by every router, describes its links/interfaces within an area. Type 2 (Network LSA) = generated by DR on multi-access networks. Type 3 (Summary LSA) = ABRs advertise inter-area routes. Type 4 (ASBR Summary) = location of ASBR. Type 5 (AS External LSA) = external routes from ASBRs. Type 7 = used in NSSA areas.

questions.question: What is a GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation) tunnel?
options: A secure encrypted VPN tunnel between two sites
A logical point-to-point tunnel that encapsulates any routed protocol inside IP packets
A QoS marking mechanism for WAN traffic
A Layer 2 tunneling protocol for VLAN extension over WAN
questions.explanation: GRE (RFC 2784) encapsulates any protocol (IPv4, IPv6, multicast) inside an IP packet, creating a virtual point-to-point link. GRE is NOT encrypted by default — combine with IPsec for security. Common uses: tunnel IPv6 over IPv4, carry multicast over point-to-point WAN (OSPF/EIGRP over GRE), connect private networks over the Internet. Overhead: 24 bytes (20 IP + 4 GRE header).

questions.question: What is route redistribution?
options: Distributing routing updates more frequently during topology changes
Importing routes from one routing protocol into another
Load-balancing traffic across multiple equal-cost paths
Advertising the same prefix from multiple routers
questions.explanation: Route redistribution allows routes learned by one routing protocol to be injected into another. Example: redistribute OSPF into EIGRP so EIGRP speakers know about OSPF routes. Requires careful metrics configuration (seed metric). Risks: routing loops and suboptimal routing in complex topologies. Also used when redistributing static routes or connected networks into a routing protocol.

questions.question: What is a key difference between IPv4 and IPv6 header structure?
options: IPv6 headers are larger with more fields than IPv4
IPv6 has a fixed 40-byte header with fewer fields; IPv4 has variable-length header (20-60 bytes)
IPv6 includes a checksum field; IPv4 does not
IPv4 supports fragmentation in routers; IPv6 does not allow router fragmentation (only at endpoints)
questions.explanation: Key IPv6 header changes vs IPv4: Fixed 40-byte header (simpler processing). No header checksum (handled by upper layers). No fragmentation by routers — IPv6 uses Path MTU Discovery; only source host can fragment. Extension headers (optional, chained). Larger addresses (128-bit vs 32-bit). Flow Label field (new) for QoS. No broadcast — uses multicast and anycast.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 address type that starts with FE80::/10?
questions.explanation: IPv6 address types: Global unicast (2000::/3) = globally routable, similar to public IPv4. Link-local (FE80::/10) = auto-configured, only valid on local link, not routed — used for neighbor discovery, routing protocols. Unique local (FC00::/7) = private, similar to RFC 1918, not globally routed. Multicast (FF00::/8) = one-to-many. Loopback (::1/128). Unspecified (::).

questions.question: What is the function of ICMPv6 Router Advertisement (RA) messages?
options: Discover the MAC address of a neighboring IPv6 host
Allow routers to advertise their presence, prefix information, and default gateway to hosts on the link
Request an IPv6 address from a DHCPv6 server
Test reachability of a neighboring router
questions.explanation: NDP Router Advertisement (RA, ICMPv6 type 134): Sent by routers periodically (or in response to RS). Contains: IPv6 prefix(es) for SLAAC, default gateway (router's link-local), MTU, M/O flags (indicates whether DHCPv6 should be used). Clients use RA to auto-configure addresses (SLAAC) and learn the default gateway without DHCP. M=1 = use DHCPv6 for address; O=1 = use DHCPv6 for other info only.

questions.question: Which STP port state forwards data frames AND builds the MAC address table?
questions.explanation: Only the Forwarding state forwards data frames and populates the MAC table. Learning state populates the MAC table but does NOT forward data frames. Listening/Blocking neither forward nor learn.

questions.question: What is the STP Bridge ID composed of?
options: Priority (4 bits) + VLAN ID (12 bits) + MAC address
questions.explanation: The Bridge ID = Bridge Priority (4 bits, default 8 = 32768) + Extended System ID/VLAN ID (12 bits) + MAC address (48 bits). Lower Bridge ID wins root bridge election.

questions.question: What port role in 802.1w (Rapid PVST+) is equivalent to the 802.1D blocking port?
questions.explanation: The Alternate port in Rapid STP replaces the non-designated/blocking port of 802.1D. It provides an alternate path to the root bridge and can transition to forwarding quickly.

questions.question: Which STP feature should be enabled on all access ports to prevent STP topology changes when devices connect?
questions.explanation: PortFast immediately transitions a port to Forwarding state, bypassing Listening and Learning. It should only be used on ports connected to end devices (not switches) to prevent loops.

questions.question: What does BPDU Guard do when a BPDU is received on a PortFast-enabled port?
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard err-disables the port when any BPDU is received — indicating an unauthorized switch was connected. This protects the STP topology from rogue switches.

questions.question: Which EtherChannel mode combination using LACP will successfully form a channel?
questions.explanation: LACP Active actively initiates negotiation. Passive waits. Active+Active or Active+Passive form a channel. Passive+Passive never negotiates. 'On' forces static bundling with no LACP.

questions.question: What is the Cisco-proprietary EtherChannel negotiation protocol?
questions.explanation: PAgP (Port Aggregation Protocol) is Cisco-proprietary. LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) is the open standard. Both negotiate EtherChannel formation dynamically.

questions.question: What must match on both ends of an EtherChannel for it to form successfully?
options: Speed, duplex, native VLAN, allowed VLANs, and spanning-tree configuration
questions.explanation: EtherChannel formation requires matching: speed, duplex, native VLAN, allowed VLANs on trunk, STP settings, and port mode (access/trunk). Mismatches cause the channel to fail.

questions.question: What is the native VLAN in 802.1Q trunking?
options: The VLAN that carries management traffic only
The VLAN whose frames are sent untagged on a trunk link
The first VLAN in the allowed list
The VLAN with the lowest ID
questions.explanation: The native VLAN sends and receives untagged frames on an 802.1Q trunk. Default is VLAN 1. Both ends of the trunk must agree on the native VLAN to avoid traffic mismatch.

questions.question: Which command adds VLAN 20 to the allowed VLANs on a trunk without removing existing VLANs?
questions.explanation: `switchport trunk allowed vlan add <vlan>` adds to the existing list. Without `add`, the command replaces the entire list with only VLAN 20.

questions.question: What is the risk of connecting a new switch with a higher VTP revision number to an existing domain?
options: The existing VLANs will be duplicated
The new switch's VLAN database will overwrite the existing domain's VLAN database
The new switch will become VTP server
VTP will disable on all switches
questions.explanation: If a new switch has a higher revision number, its VLAN database overwrites all other switches in the VTP domain — potentially deleting all production VLANs.

questions.question: Which 802.11 standard operates ONLY in the 5 GHz band?
questions.explanation: 802.11a operates only in 5 GHz at up to 54 Mbps. 802.11b and 802.11g use 2.4 GHz. 802.11n uses both bands.

questions.question: What is the maximum theoretical throughput of 802.11ac (Wave 1)?
questions.explanation: 802.11ac Wave 1 supports up to ~1.3 Gbps using 80 MHz channels and 3 spatial streams. 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) reaches ~9.6 Gbps. 802.11n reaches ~600 Mbps.

questions.question: What is the purpose of CAPWAP in a wireless LAN architecture?
options: Encrypts data between wireless clients and the AP
Provides a tunnel between lightweight APs and the WLC for management and data
Assigns IP addresses to wireless clients
Manages VLAN assignments on wired switches
questions.explanation: CAPWAP (Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points) uses UDP 5246 (control) and 5247 (data) to tunnel traffic between lightweight APs and the WLC.

questions.question: In a split-MAC wireless architecture, which functions are handled by the WLC?
options: RF management, security policies, roaming, QoS
questions.explanation: In split-MAC, the WLC handles: authentication, RF management, QoS, roaming, security policies. The AP handles: 802.11 timing (beacons, probe responses) and frame transmission.

questions.question: What is a rogue access point?
options: An unauthorized AP connected to the corporate network
An AP that does not support WPA2
questions.explanation: A rogue AP is an unauthorized access point connected to the corporate network (often by an employee). It bypasses corporate security controls and may allow unauthorized access.

questions.question: How many non-overlapping channels does the 2.4 GHz band support in North America?
questions.explanation: 2.4 GHz has 11 channels in NA but only 3 are non-overlapping: 1, 6, and 11. Adjacent channels overlap and cause co-channel or adjacent-channel interference.

questions.question: What is an evil twin attack in wireless networking?
options: A denial of service attack using deauthentication frames
A rogue AP configured with the same SSID as a legitimate AP to intercept client traffic
questions.explanation: An evil twin AP mimics a legitimate AP's SSID and BSSID to trick clients into connecting. The attacker can then perform man-in-the-middle attacks on the unsuspecting clients.

questions.question: Which wireless technology does 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) introduce for improved efficiency in dense environments?
questions.explanation: 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) introduces OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access), which divides channels into smaller sub-channels (RUs) to serve multiple clients simultaneously.

questions.question: What action does a switch take when it receives a frame with a destination MAC address NOT in its MAC address table?
options: Sends the frame to the default gateway
Floods the frame out all ports except the receiving port
questions.explanation: When the destination MAC is unknown, the switch floods the frame out all ports in the same VLAN except the port it was received on. This is 'unknown unicast flooding'.

questions.question: What is a MAC address table overflow (CAM table overflow) attack?
options: Sending large frames to exhaust switch memory
Flooding the switch with thousands of frames with random source MACs, causing it to flood all traffic
A denial-of-service attack on the spanning tree
questions.explanation: A CAM overflow attack sends frames with thousands of fake source MACs, filling the MAC table. The switch then floods all frames like a hub, allowing traffic capture.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies which VLANs are active and which ports are assigned to each VLAN?
questions.explanation: `show vlan brief` displays all VLANs, their status, and assigned access ports. `show interfaces trunk` shows trunk ports and their allowed/active VLANs.

questions.question: What is required to enable router-on-a-stick inter-VLAN routing?
options: Subinterfaces with 802.1Q encapsulation on the router and a trunk port on the switch
A separate physical interface per VLAN on the router
OSPF between the router and switch
questions.explanation: Router-on-a-stick uses one physical router interface with multiple subinterfaces (e.g., Gi0/0.10, Gi0/0.20), each with `encapsulation dot1q <vlan>` and an IP address. The switch port must be a trunk.

questions.question: What Layer 3 switch command enables IP routing between SVIs?
questions.explanation: `ip routing` enables Layer 3 forwarding on a multilayer switch. Combined with SVIs (`interface vlan <id>` + IP address), the switch can route between VLANs without an external router.

questions.question: What is LLDP and why is it preferred over CDP in multi-vendor environments?
options: Link Layer Discovery Protocol; it is Cisco-proprietary
Link Layer Discovery Protocol; it is an IEEE 802.1AB standard supported by all vendors
Layer 2 Link Discovery Protocol; it works at Layer 3
Loop Link Detection Protocol; prevents STP loops
questions.explanation: LLDP (IEEE 802.1AB) is the vendor-neutral equivalent of Cisco's CDP. It discovers directly connected neighbor devices and their capabilities across all vendor platforms.

questions.question: What command manually sets a switch as the root bridge for VLAN 10?
options: Both A and B are valid
questions.explanation: Both `spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary` (sets priority to 24576 or lower to win) and `spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096` (manually set low priority) can make a switch the root bridge.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies EtherChannel status and shows which physical interfaces are bundled?
questions.explanation: `show etherchannel summary` shows port-channel groups, protocol (LACP/PAgP/static), and member interface status (P=in port-channel, I=standalone, D=down).

questions.question: What is the range of extended VLANs in Cisco IOS?
questions.explanation: Normal VLANs: 1-1005 (stored in vlan.dat). Extended VLANs: 1006-4094 (stored in running-config when VTP mode is transparent or off). VLAN 1 is the default; 1002-1005 are reserved for Token Ring/FDDI.

questions.question: What is an Infrastructure BSS wireless topology?
options: Direct device-to-device communication without an AP
Devices connect to an AP (Access Point) which serves as the central connection point
A wired and wireless hybrid network
questions.explanation: Infrastructure BSS (Basic Service Set) has all devices connecting through an AP. The AP manages medium access and provides connection to the wired network. IBSS (ad-hoc) is direct device-to-device.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using SVIs for inter-VLAN routing compared to router-on-a-stick?
options: SVIs route traffic within the switch at hardware speed (ASIC), providing much higher throughput than router-on-a-stick
questions.explanation: Layer 3 switches route between SVIs using dedicated hardware (ASICs) at wire speed — potentially millions of pps. Router-on-a-stick funnels all inter-VLAN traffic through a single physical link, creating a bottleneck.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies the MAC address table on a switch?
questions.explanation: `show mac address-table` displays the switch's MAC address table, showing which MAC addresses are learned on which ports and VLANs. Can filter with `show mac address-table dynamic`.

questions.question: What is an ESS (Extended Service Set) in wireless networking?
options: A single AP with its associated clients
Multiple APs with the same SSID connected to the same wired distribution system, allowing seamless roaming
An ad-hoc network between two devices
questions.explanation: An ESS consists of multiple BSSs (APs) sharing the same SSID and connected via a wired distribution system. Clients roam between APs without reconfiguring network settings.

questions.question: What is the STP port cost for a 1 Gbps interface using the IEEE 802.1D-2004 long path cost?
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1D-2004 long path costs: 10 Mbps=2,000,000, 100 Mbps=200,000, 1 Gbps=20,000, 10 Gbps=2,000. Original 802.1D short method: 10M=100, 100M=19, 1G=4, 10G=2.

questions.question: What WLAN architecture is recommended for large enterprise deployments with centralized management?
options: Lightweight APs with a WLC (Wireless LAN Controller)
questions.explanation: Large enterprise WLANs use lightweight APs with a centralized WLC for scalable management, consistent policy enforcement, seamless roaming, and RF optimization across hundreds of APs.

questions.question: Which command verifies the operational trunk status and active VLANs on a trunk interface?
questions.explanation: `show interfaces trunk` shows: trunk ports, encapsulation (802.1Q), allowed VLANs, active VLANs, and which VLANs are in STP forwarding state on each trunk.

questions.question: What is the Root Port in STP?
options: The port on the Root Bridge that leads to the rest of the network
The port on each non-root switch that provides the lowest-cost path back to the Root Bridge
The port blocked to prevent loops
The port connected to the default gateway
questions.explanation: Each non-root switch has exactly one Root Port — the port with the lowest-cost path to the Root Bridge. On the Root Bridge, all ports are Designated Ports (no Root Port).

questions.question: What determines which port is blocked on a non-root switch segment with two paths of equal cost to the root?
options: The port with the lower port number is blocked
The port connected to the switch with the lower Bridge ID is the Designated Port; the other is blocked
questions.explanation: When costs are equal, STP compares sending Bridge IDs. The segment's Designated Port is on the switch with the lower Bridge ID. The other switch's port toward that segment becomes Non-Designated (blocked).

questions.question: What IOS commands configure an interface as an access port in VLAN 10?
questions.explanation: Two commands: (1) `switchport mode access` sets the port to access mode (not trunk). (2) `switchport access vlan 10` assigns it to VLAN 10. Without the first command, the port may negotiate trunk mode via DTP.

questions.question: What wireless security vulnerability does TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol) address compared to WEP?
options: TKIP adds per-packet key mixing and message integrity check (MIC) to fix WEP's weak static keys
TKIP eliminates the need for a passphrase
TKIP uses AES instead of RC4
questions.explanation: TKIP was a software fix for WEP-based hardware: adds per-packet key rotation, MIC (Michael), and replay protection. However, TKIP (used in WPA) is still RC4-based and is now deprecated — use AES/CCMP (WPA2).

questions.question: Which STP timer controls how long a port waits in the Listening and Learning states?
questions.explanation: Forward Delay (default 15 seconds) is the time a port spends in each of the Listening and Learning states. Total STP convergence without PortFast = 30 seconds in L/L + Max Age for topology change detection.

questions.question: What WLAN component handles roaming between APs without client re-authentication in a WLC-based architecture?
options: The WLC manages roaming, maintaining the client session as it moves between APs
questions.explanation: In centralized WLC architecture, the WLC tracks client sessions. When a client roams to another AP, the WLC seamlessly transfers the session without requiring re-authentication (fast/seamless roaming).

questions.question: What is a 'voice VLAN' and why is it used on access ports?
options: A VLAN dedicated to router management
A separate VLAN configured on an access port to carry tagged IP phone traffic while the port also carries untagged data traffic
A VLAN for wireless voice clients only
questions.explanation: A voice VLAN allows one switch port to carry both: untagged data (PC) in the access VLAN and tagged voice traffic in the voice VLAN. Command: `switchport voice vlan <id>`. Enables QoS separation of voice/data.

questions.question: What is a Designated Port in STP?
options: The port on each switch closest to the Root Bridge
The one forwarding port on each network segment that provides the lowest-cost path toward the Root Bridge
The port connected to the default gateway
questions.explanation: Each LAN segment has one Designated Port — the port on that segment providing the best (lowest cost) path toward the root bridge. All ports on the Root Bridge are Designated. Only one per segment forwards traffic.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of active physical links in an LACP EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: LACP supports up to 8 active member links in an EtherChannel (with up to 8 standby/hot-standby links in reserve). PAgP supports up to 8 active links. Total = 16 bundled, 8 active.

questions.question: What does WPA2 use for encryption in enterprise (802.1X) mode?
options: AES with CCMP (Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol)
