The port is immediately placed in err-disabled state
The port transitions to STP listening state
The switch elects a new root bridge
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard places a PortFast port into err-disabled state immediately upon receiving any BPDU. This prevents rogue switches from disrupting the STP topology. The port remains err-disabled until manually re-enabled or errdisable recovery is configured.
hints: BPDU Guard + PortFast = protection against unauthorized switches
err-disabled requires: shutdown then no shutdown to recover (or errdisable recovery)

questions.question: "show interfaces trunk" shows Fa0/1 trunking but VLAN 20 is absent from "VLANs allowed and active in management domain". Why?
options: VLAN 20 does not exist in the local VLAN database on this switch
The trunk has "switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 20" configured
Both B and C could independently cause this
questions.explanation: "VLANs allowed and active in management domain" shows VLANs that are both allowed on the trunk AND exist in the local VLAN database. Missing from this column means either: (1) removed from allowed list, or (2) not created with "vlan 20" locally. Either condition produces this result.
hints: Three trunk columns: allowed, allowed and active, allowed and active in STP
A VLAN must exist locally AND be in the allowed list to appear active

questions.question: Switch A Gi0/1: "switchport mode dynamic desirable". Switch B Gi0/1: "switchport mode access". What is the resulting port state?
options: Trunk — dynamic desirable always forms a trunk
Access — access mode does not participate in DTP and rejects trunk formation
Dynamic — both ports remain in negotiation
questions.explanation: "dynamic desirable" actively sends DTP frames to form a trunk. However "access" is a fixed state that does not respond to DTP. The result is both ports in access mode — the trunk does not form.
hints: DTP modes: auto (passive), desirable (active), trunk (forced), access (no trunk)
Access mode ignores DTP frames — it will not become a trunk

questions.question: "channel-group 1 mode active" on both ends forms an EtherChannel. If one side changes to "mode on", what happens?
options: LACP; changing to "on" breaks the channel
PAgP; "on" is valid with PAgP active
LACP; "on" is compatible with "active" and the bundle stays up
LACP; "on" requires the other side to also be "on"
questions.explanation: "active" uses LACP (802.3ad). "mode on" forces EtherChannel unconditionally with no protocol. LACP "active" sends LACP PDUs and expects LACP responses. "on" neither sends nor responds to LACP PDUs. Mixing "active" (expects LACP) with "on" (ignores LACP) causes the channel to fail.
hints: mode active/passive = LACP; mode desirable/auto = PAgP
mode on = no protocol; incompatible with LACP or PAgP modes

questions.question: A port shows err-disabled. What two commands enable automatic recovery WITHOUT manual shutdown/no shutdown?
options: errdisable recovery cause bpduguard (alone is sufficient)
Both: "errdisable recovery cause <reason>" AND "errdisable recovery interval <seconds>"
questions.explanation: Automatic errdisable recovery requires: (1) "errdisable recovery cause <reason>" to enable recovery for that specific cause, AND (2) "errdisable recovery interval <seconds>" to set the timer (default 300s). Without specifying the cause, recovery is not active for that cause.
hints: Must enable recovery per cause (e.g. bpduguard, psecure-violation)
Default recovery interval = 300 seconds

questions.question: A non-root switch has two paths to root: Gi0/1 (cost 4) and Gi0/2 (cost 19). Both operational. What RSTP role does Gi0/2 have?
options: Alternate port — backup path to root
Designated port — forwards toward non-root segments
Backup port — redundancy on the same segment
questions.explanation: RSTP alternate port: a port that receives BPDUs from another switch and provides a backup path to the root. Gi0/2 with higher cost (19 vs 4) is the inferior path — it becomes the alternate port, ready for instant failover if Gi0/1 fails.
hints: Root port = best path to root (lowest cost)
Alternate port = RSTP backup to root port (was blocking in classic STP)

questions.question: Port security: maximum 2, violation restrict, sticky. A third device connects. What happens?
options: Frames from the third MAC are dropped; syslog/SNMP alert sent; port stays up
Oldest secure MAC is removed and new one is learned
questions.explanation: "restrict" mode: frames from violating MACs are dropped, a syslog message and optional SNMP trap are generated, violation counter increments, port stays up. Compare: "protect" drops silently; "shutdown" err-disables the port.
hints: Three violation modes: protect (drop silently), restrict (drop+log), shutdown (err-disable)
Only shutdown mode takes the port down

questions.question: VTP version 2 is running. A switch from another network (VTP revision number 50) is connected; current network highest revision is 30. What happens?
options: No risk — lower revision prevents synchronization
The new switch overwrites all switches with its VLAN database — higher revision always wins
The new switch is blocked by VTP authentication
VTP v2 uses MAC-based precedence, ignoring revision numbers
questions.explanation: VTP uses the revision number to determine the authoritative VLAN database. Higher revision always wins. A stale switch with a higher revision number overwrites the network's VLAN database, potentially deleting production VLANs and causing an outage. Best practice: reset revision to 0 before connecting a new switch (change to transparent mode and back).
hints: VTP revision: higher number = authoritative
Always reset revision to 0 before connecting a new VTP server

questions.question: What is the STP port cost of a 10 Gbps interface using default (802.1D short) cost values?
questions.explanation: Default Cisco 802.1D short STP costs: 10 Mbps=100, 100 Mbps=19, 1 Gbps=4, 10 Gbps=2. Lower cost = preferred path. Use "spanning-tree pathcost method long" for IEEE 802.1D-2004 long costs (10G=2,000).
hints: Original 802.1D short costs: 10G=2, 1G=4, 100M=19, 10M=100
Longer path = higher cost = less preferred

questions.question: Switch A: "switchport mode trunk, switchport nonegotiate". Connected switch runs default DTP. What is the result?
options: Trunk forms — forced trunk accepts DTP from the other side
No trunk — nonegotiate stops sending DTP; the other switch in dynamic auto stays in access mode
Both sides must have nonegotiate for trunking
questions.explanation: Actually, "mode trunk" + "nonegotiate" means this side is a forced trunk and does not SEND DTP. The other switch in "dynamic auto" passively waits for DTP. Since it receives no DTP offer, it stays in access mode. The trunk does NOT form because dynamic auto requires a DTP offer to transition to trunk. The result is a mismatch — the forced trunk side thinks it's trunking but the other is in access.
hints: nonegotiate: no DTP frames sent from this side
dynamic auto = passive; without DTP, stays in access mode

questions.question: A switch receives a frame with an unknown unicast destination MAC. What does it do?
options: Drops the frame — unknown MACs are not forwarded
Floods the frame out all ports in the VLAN except the ingress port
Sends an ARP request to find the destination
Forwards it to the default gateway
questions.explanation: Unknown unicast flooding: when the destination MAC is not in the CAM table, the switch floods the frame out all ports in the same VLAN except the ingress port (source port). This allows the destination device to receive the frame and its reply populates the CAM table.
hints: Known unicast = forwarded to single port
Unknown unicast = flooded within VLAN (like broadcast)

questions.question: What is the purpose of 802.1Q ISL (Inter-Switch Link) vs 802.1Q trunking on modern Cisco equipment?
options: ISL is the current standard; 802.1Q is legacy
802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag into the Ethernet frame; ISL encapsulates the entire frame with a new header — ISL is deprecated
Both are identical — the names refer to the same protocol
questions.explanation: 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag into the original Ethernet frame between the source MAC and EtherType fields. ISL (Cisco proprietary) encapsulates the entire original frame inside a new ISL frame. ISL is deprecated — modern Cisco IOS only supports 802.1Q trunking.
hints: 802.1Q = tag inserted in original frame (4 bytes)
ISL = full frame encapsulation (Cisco proprietary, deprecated)

questions.question: STP TCN (Topology Change Notification) is sent when a port transitions. What does a switch do upon receiving a TCN BPDU from a neighbor?
options: Immediately removes all MAC table entries
Reduces the MAC address aging timer to forward_delay (15s) to flush stale entries faster
Triggers a new root bridge election
Sends TCN to all ports including non-root ports
questions.explanation: Upon receiving a TCN, the switch reduces the MAC address aging timer from 300s to forward_delay (default 15s). This causes stale MAC entries to be flushed sooner, preventing traffic from being sent to ports where devices may have moved. The switch also forwards the TCN toward the root bridge.
hints: TCN causes MAC table to age faster (300s → forward_delay 15s)
Stale MACs flush quickly after a topology change

questions.question: RSTP port states differ from 802.1D STP. Which 802.1D states are combined into RSTP "discarding" state?
questions.explanation: RSTP simplifies STP port states. "Discarding" combines three 802.1D states: Disabled (administratively down), Blocking (active but not forwarding — waiting for STP convergence), and Listening (transitional, sending/receiving BPDUs but not forwarding data). RSTP retains Learning and Forwarding.
hints: RSTP has 3 states: Discarding, Learning, Forwarding
Discarding = 802.1D disabled + blocking + listening combined

questions.question: You connect a host to a port configured with "switchport port-security maximum 1 sticky". The host communicates normally. Later the host is replaced. What happens when the new host connects?
options: New host is immediately allowed — sticky learns the new MAC
Violation occurs — default shutdown mode err-disables the port
Violation occurs — old sticky MAC persists in running config and the new MAC violates
The sticky MAC updates automatically when a new host connects
questions.explanation: Sticky MAC addresses are written to the running config and persist as long as they are not manually removed. When the port learns MAC X via sticky, it keeps MAC X as the secure MAC. A second device with a different MAC violates port security. The default violation mode is shutdown (err-disable). The sticky MAC must be manually cleared ("no switchport port-security mac-address sticky <mac>") to allow a new host.
hints: Sticky MACs persist in running config until manually removed
Default violation mode = shutdown (err-disable)

questions.question: A switch has "spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 0". What bridge priority does it actually use?
options: 4096 — the lowest valid priority increment in IOS
0 — valid only if root guard is disabled
Priority 0 is invalid and the command is rejected
questions.explanation: Cisco IOS requires VLAN STP priority to be a multiple of 4096. Valid values: 0, 4096, 8192, ..., 61440. "priority 0" is valid and sets the extended system ID correctly. The actual bridge priority value used is 0 (plus the VLAN ID in the extended system ID field). So "priority 0" is accepted and results in bridge priority 0 + VLAN ID = 10 for VLAN 10.
hints: Priority must be multiple of 4096 (0, 4096, 8192...)
Priority 0 is valid — results in the lowest possible BID for that VLAN

questions.question: An 802.1Q trunk has the native VLAN set to VLAN 99. What is true about traffic on VLAN 99 over this trunk?
options: VLAN 99 frames are tagged with 802.1Q tag ID 99
VLAN 99 frames are sent untagged (no 802.1Q header)
VLAN 99 is blocked on 802.1Q trunks by default
VLAN 99 frames use ISL encapsulation automatically
questions.explanation: The native VLAN is the one VLAN whose frames traverse an 802.1Q trunk WITHOUT a VLAN tag. All other VLANs are tagged. This means any untagged frame received on a trunk is assumed to belong to the native VLAN. Misconfiguring native VLANs between switches causes frames to be placed in the wrong VLAN.
hints: Native VLAN = untagged VLAN on 802.1Q trunks
Untagged frames arriving on a trunk are assigned to the native VLAN

questions.question: A switch MAC table is full. An unknown unicast frame arrives. What does the switch do, and what attack exploits this behavior?
options: Drops the frame; this is normal STP convergence behavior
Floods the frame out all ports; MAC flooding attack exploits this to capture traffic
Sends ICMP unreachable; denial-of-service attack uses this
Crashes the switch; buffer overflow attack targets this
questions.explanation: When the CAM table is full, any unknown unicast frame is flooded (the switch cannot store new MAC entries). An attacker deliberately fills the CAM table with fake MACs using tools like macof, causing all unicast traffic to be flooded — effectively turning the switch into a hub and allowing passive sniffing of all traffic. Port security mitigates this by limiting MAC entries per port.
hints: Full CAM table = all unknown unicast flooded (switch acts like hub)
MAC flooding attack: fill CAM to force flooding, then sniff traffic

questions.question: EtherChannel load balancing is configured as "port-channel load-balance src-dst-ip". Two flows: Flow A and Flow B share the same source IP but have different destination IPs. Are they load-balanced?
options: No — same source IP means same hash, same link always used
Yes — different destination IPs produce different hash outputs, distributing across links
No — EtherChannel only balances based on destination MAC
Yes — EtherChannel always distributes equally regardless of configuration
questions.explanation: With "src-dst-ip", the hash uses BOTH source AND destination IP. Flow A and Flow B have the same source IP but different destination IPs, so the combined hash differs — they may be assigned to different links. If only "src-ip" were used, both flows would use the same link (same source IP).
hints: src-dst-ip = hash of both source and destination IP
Different dst IPs with same src IP = different hash → may use different links

questions.question: "show spanning-tree vlan 10" shows port Gi0/2 in BLK (blocking) state. What type of traffic does this port process?
options: No traffic at all — the port is physically shut down
BPDUs only — it receives and processes STP BPDUs but does not forward data frames
All traffic — BLK only means the port is in backup mode
Data frames from VLAN 10 only; BPDUs are blocked
questions.explanation: A blocking STP port still receives and processes BPDUs — this is how it monitors the topology for changes and participates in STP. It does NOT forward data frames or learn MAC addresses. The port stays physically active at Layer 2 for STP but is logically blocked for data.
hints: Blocking port = no data forwarding, no MAC learning
Blocking port DOES process BPDUs (monitors topology)

questions.question: 802.1X port-based authentication: what are the three roles in the 802.1X framework?
questions.explanation: 802.1X uses three roles: Supplicant = the end device requesting access (uses EAP). Authenticator = the network device (switch/wireless AP) controlling port access, forwarding credentials via RADIUS. Authentication server = validates credentials (typically a RADIUS server like Cisco ISE). The port remains unauthorized until authentication succeeds.
hints: Supplicant = end device (EAP client)
Authenticator = switch/AP; Authentication server = RADIUS

questions.question: PVST+ (Per-VLAN Spanning Tree Plus) runs a separate STP instance per VLAN. What is the primary benefit over classic 802.1D?
options: Faster convergence — PVST+ uses RSTP timers
Load balancing — different VLANs can have different root bridges and use different trunk paths
Less CPU overhead — one instance handles all VLANs
Better security — each VLAN has isolated STP topology
questions.explanation: PVST+ allows each VLAN to have a separate STP instance with its own root bridge. This enables load balancing across trunk links: VLAN 10 traffic can use the left trunk (Switch A as root) while VLAN 20 uses the right trunk (Switch B as root). Classic 802.1D has one instance for all VLANs, leaving redundant links always blocked.
hints: PVST+ = one STP instance per VLAN
Different root bridges per VLAN enables load balancing across redundant links

questions.question: LACP "passive" mode on both sides of an EtherChannel. What happens?
options: Bundle forms — passive responds to LACP PDUs
Bundle does not form — both wait for the other to initiate
One side auto-upgrades to active mode
questions.explanation: LACP "passive" waits for LACP PDUs before responding. If both sides are passive, neither initiates — no LACP PDUs are sent, and the bundle never forms. At least one side must be "active" (sends LACP PDUs) for the EtherChannel to negotiate.
hints: passive = wait for LACP; active = send LACP
passive + passive = no negotiation = no bundle

questions.question: A switch port "show interface Gi0/1" reports "input errors: 5432, CRC: 5432". What is the most likely cause?
options: The port is in half-duplex and experiencing collisions
A duplex mismatch — one side is full-duplex, other is half-duplex, causing late collisions and CRC errors
The cable is too long, exceeding Ethernet distance specifications
Port security violation is corrupting frames
questions.explanation: CRC errors combined with input errors and late collisions are classic symptoms of a duplex mismatch. The full-duplex side transmits while the half-duplex side is also transmitting, causing the half-duplex side to detect a collision and corrupt the frame. The receiving side sees CRC errors. Fix: set both sides to the same duplex (preferably full-duplex with explicit configuration or auto-negotiation).
hints: CRC errors + late collisions = duplex mismatch
Auto-negotiation failure can cause one side to be half-duplex

questions.question: Inter-VLAN routing via router-on-a-stick: the router has subinterfaces Gi0/0.10 and Gi0/0.20 with "encapsulation dot1q 10" and "encapsulation dot1q 20". The physical interface has no IP. Why?
options: The physical interface must have an IP — this configuration is invalid
The physical interface acts as the trunk to the switch; IP addresses are on the subinterfaces for each VLAN — the physical interface itself does not need an IP
The physical interface uses ISL so no IP is needed
IP is auto-assigned to the physical interface from the subinterfaces
questions.explanation: In router-on-a-stick, the physical interface is configured as a trunk (no IP, no switchport) and the switch connects to it via an 802.1Q trunk port. Each subinterface (Gi0/0.10, Gi0/0.20) terminates one VLAN and has an IP address that serves as the default gateway for that VLAN. The physical interface is just the trunk carrier — it does not route itself.
hints: Physical interface = trunk carrier (no IP needed)
Each subinterface = one VLAN gateway; "encapsulation dot1q N" tags the VLAN

questions.question: "show interfaces Gi0/1 trunk" shows the port as "trunking" but no VLANs in "VLANs in spanning tree forwarding state and not pruned". What does this indicate?
options: STP has blocked all VLANs on this trunk — the trunk is up but no data flows
VTP pruning has removed all VLANs from this trunk
The allowed VLAN list is empty on this trunk
Both VTP pruning and an empty allowed list could cause this
questions.explanation: "VLANs in spanning tree forwarding state and not pruned" shows active, forwarding, non-pruned VLANs. An empty list means either: (1) all VLANs are STP-blocked on this trunk, (2) VTP pruning has removed all VLANs, or (3) the allowed VLAN list excludes all VLANs. All three can produce this result independently.
hints: Third column = VLANs that are: allowed AND active AND STP forwarding AND not pruned
Empty = one or more filtering conditions eliminated all VLANs

questions.question: A switch has "vtp mode transparent". What does VTP transparent mode do?
options: Participates in VTP and synchronizes VLAN database from the VTP server
Does not participate in VTP synchronization but forwards VTP advertisements to other switches; maintains its own local VLAN database
Blocks all VTP advertisements — acts as a VTP firewall
Requires manual VLAN creation; same as VTP off mode
questions.explanation: VTP transparent mode: the switch does NOT synchronize its VLAN database from VTP servers (it keeps its own local database). It DOES forward VTP advertisements received on trunk ports to other switches (passes them through without modifying). This makes it "transparent" — it passes VTP traffic without participating. VTP off is similar but does not forward VTP advertisements.
hints: Transparent = local VLAN database + forwards (but ignores) VTP advertisements
VTP off = same as transparent but does not forward VTP advertisements

questions.question: Which STP port state comes immediately after listening in classic 802.1D?
questions.explanation: Classic 802.1D STP port states in order: Disabled → Blocking → Listening (forward_delay timer, 15s) → Learning (forward_delay timer, 15s) → Forwarding. In Listening, the port processes BPDUs and participates in STP election but does not forward data or learn MACs. In Learning, it still does not forward data but starts learning MAC addresses.
hints: 802.1D states: Disabled→Blocking→Listening→Learning→Forwarding
Listening and Learning each last forward_delay seconds (default 15s)

questions.question: What is the purpose of CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) and at which OSI layer does it operate?
options: Layer 3 routing protocol between Cisco devices
Layer 2 (data link); discovers directly connected Cisco devices and shares platform, IP, capabilities, and interface info
Layer 4 session management between Cisco routers
questions.explanation: CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) operates at Layer 2 (independent of Network layer protocol). It sends multicast frames every 60 seconds to 0100.0CCC.CCCC. Each device learns: neighboring device hostname, model, IOS version, IP address, capabilities, native VLAN, duplex. CDP is proprietary. LLDP (IEEE 802.1AB) is the open standard equivalent.
hints: CDP = Layer 2 proprietary Cisco discovery
Shows directly connected neighbors: model, IOS, IPs, capabilities

questions.question: LLDP is enabled. "show lldp neighbors detail" shows a neighbor with capability "B,R". What does this mean?
options: B=Bridge (switch), R=Router — the neighbor is a Layer 3 switch or router
questions.explanation: LLDP system capabilities: B = Bridge (Layer 2 switch), R = Router, T = Telephone, C = DOCSIS Cable Device, W = WLAN Access Point, P = Repeater, S = Station. "B,R" indicates the neighbor has both bridging and routing capabilities — a Layer 3 switch or a router with a switch module.
hints: LLDP capabilities: B=Bridge, R=Router, T=Telephone
B,R = Layer 3 switch (routes and switches)

questions.question: A switch interface shows "connected" in "show interfaces status" but "show spanning-tree vlan 10" does not list the port. Why?
options: STP is disabled globally on the switch
The port is not assigned to VLAN 10 — STP only lists ports participating in that VLAN
The port speed is incompatible with STP
questions.explanation: "show spanning-tree vlan 10" only displays ports that are members of VLAN 10 (access ports in VLAN 10 or trunk ports carrying VLAN 10). If a port is in VLAN 20 or the trunk does not carry VLAN 10, it will not appear in the VLAN 10 STP output.
hints: STP per-VLAN: only shows ports in that VLAN
Port not in VLAN 10 = absent from "show spanning-tree vlan 10"

questions.question: A switch has "vtp mode client". Can you create VLANs on it?
options: Yes — VTP clients can create VLANs locally
No — VTP clients receive VLAN database from server; local VLAN creation is rejected
Yes — but only extended range VLANs (1006-4094)
Yes — if VTP pruning is disabled
questions.explanation: VTP clients: VLAN database is received from and synchronized with the VTP server. You CANNOT create, modify, or delete VLANs on a VTP client — the commands are rejected. VTP clients do save the received VLAN database to flash (vlan.dat). VTP transparent mode allows local VLAN creation without syncing.
hints: VTP client: receives VLAN database only; cannot create/modify VLANs
Use VTP transparent or VTP off for local VLAN management

questions.question: What is the Cisco default VLAN for untagged traffic on a trunk port?
options: VLAN 1 — the default native VLAN on Cisco IOS
questions.explanation: Cisco IOS default native VLAN on 802.1Q trunk ports is VLAN 1. Untagged frames received on a trunk are assigned to the native VLAN. Best practice: change the native VLAN to an unused VLAN (e.g. VLAN 999) and ensure both sides match to prevent VLAN hopping attacks and misassignment of untagged traffic.
hints: Default native VLAN = 1 on Cisco
Change to unused VLAN for security (mitigate VLAN hopping)

questions.question: STP is calculating the root port on a non-root switch. Two paths exist: path A (cost 4) and path B (cost 4, tie). How is the tiebreaker resolved?
options: Lowest sender Bridge ID → lowest sender port priority → lowest sender port ID
questions.explanation: STP root port tiebreaker order (all for the path toward root): 1. Lowest root path cost. 2. Lowest sender (upstream) Bridge ID. 3. Lowest sender port priority (default 128). 4. Lowest sender port ID (port number). These tiebreakers apply in sequence — move to the next only if previous is equal.
hints: Tiebreakers in order: lowest root cost → lowest sender BID → lowest sender port priority → lowest sender port ID
Port priority default = 128; lower = preferred

questions.question: What is the purpose of "switchport trunk native vlan 999" and "vlan 999" — why configure both?
options: Only "switchport trunk native vlan 999" is needed; "vlan 999" is for a different feature
Both are required: the native VLAN must exist in the VLAN database (vlan 999) before traffic is placed in it; without both, untagged traffic may be dropped or assigned incorrectly
Only "vlan 999" is needed; native VLAN changes automatically
VLAN 999 is reserved and cannot be used as native
questions.explanation: "switchport trunk native vlan 999" sets the native VLAN for untagged traffic. For the VLAN to be active, it must also exist in the local VLAN database ("vlan 999"). If the native VLAN is not in the VLAN database, the port will place untagged frames into a non-existent VLAN — they will be dropped by STP (inactive VLAN).
hints: Native VLAN must be set AND created in VLAN database
Untagged frames assigned to a non-existent VLAN are dropped

questions.question: What is the difference between PortFast and BPDU Guard?
options: PortFast and BPDU Guard are the same feature
PortFast skips listening/learning states for faster access port activation; BPDU Guard disables the port if a BPDU is received (protects against rogue switches on PortFast ports)
BPDU Guard enables rapid RSTP convergence; PortFast blocks unauthorized BPDUs
PortFast is for trunk ports; BPDU Guard is for access ports only
questions.explanation: PortFast: transitions access ports directly to forwarding (skips 802.1D listening+learning or RSTP learning delay). Suitable for end-device ports. BPDU Guard: if a PortFast port receives a BPDU (indicating a switch connected instead of a host), err-disables the port immediately. They work together: PortFast speeds up, BPDU Guard protects.
hints: PortFast: skip STP transitions for end-device ports
BPDU Guard: err-disable if BPDU received (rogue switch protection)

questions.question: An access port has "switchport access vlan 30". The VLAN 30 is deleted from the VLAN database. What happens to traffic on that port?
options: Traffic continues — the port remembers VLAN 30
The port is placed in VLAN 1 automatically
The port becomes inactive — it is associated with a non-existent VLAN and traffic is dropped
questions.explanation: If the VLAN assigned to an access port is deleted from the VLAN database, that port becomes inactive (shown as "inactive" in "show interfaces status"). Traffic is dropped because the VLAN does not exist. The port assignment remains (still shows "switchport access vlan 30") but no traffic flows until VLAN 30 is recreated.
hints: Deleted VLAN = access port becomes inactive
Port assignment remains; recreate the VLAN to restore connectivity

questions.question: "switchport mode trunk" — does this generate DTP frames?
options: Yes — trunk mode actively sends DTP to negotiate trunking with the neighbor (unless "nonegotiate" is added)
Only if the neighbor is in "auto" mode
DTP frames are sent only on access ports
questions.explanation: "switchport mode trunk" sets the port to unconditional trunk AND by default continues to send DTP frames to help the other side also become a trunk. To fully disable DTP (recommended for security on trunks to non-Cisco devices or static configs), add "switchport nonegotiate". "mode trunk" + "nonegotiate" = forced trunk, no DTP frames.
hints: trunk mode still sends DTP by default
Add "nonegotiate" to stop DTP frames: "switchport nonegotiate"

questions.question: Root Guard is configured on a switch port. What happens if a superior BPDU is received on that port?
options: The switch elects a new root based on the superior BPDU
The port is placed in root-inconsistent state and stops forwarding — the superior BPDU is ignored
The switch sends a TCN to the current root
questions.explanation: Root Guard prevents designated ports from becoming root ports. If a superior BPDU (with a lower BID than the current root) is received on a Root Guard port, the port enters "root-inconsistent" STP state (BLK) and drops all traffic until the superior BPDUs stop. It automatically recovers once superior BPDUs cease — unlike BPDU Guard which requires manual recovery.
hints: Root Guard: prevents topology changes from unauthorized root elections
root-inconsistent = blocking; auto-recovers when superior BPDUs stop

questions.question: What is Rapid PVST+ and how does it improve convergence over PVST+?
options: Rapid PVST+ uses faster timers (5s max-age) vs PVST+ (20s)
Rapid PVST+ (IEEE 802.1w per-VLAN) uses edge ports, alternate ports, and proposal/agreement mechanism for near-instant convergence vs PVST+ (802.1D) which relies on timers (30-50s)
Rapid PVST+ only works on GigE links; PVST+ works on all speeds
Rapid PVST+ eliminates the need for root bridge election
questions.explanation: Rapid PVST+ implements RSTP (802.1w) per VLAN. Key improvements: edge ports (PortFast equivalent) go straight to forwarding. Proposal/agreement handshake between switches enables link-by-link rapid transitions without waiting for max-age + 2×forward_delay (up to 50s in 802.1D). Alternate and backup ports provide instant failover without reconvergence.
hints: Rapid PVST+ = RSTP per VLAN (802.1w implementation)
Proposal/agreement = near-instant convergence vs timer-based 802.1D (up to 50s)

questions.question: What is the maximum number of VLANs supported in a Cisco switch VLAN database (normal range)?
questions.explanation: Normal VLAN range: 1–1005 (1-4 and 1006 are reserved by Cisco; usable: 2-1001). Extended VLAN range: 1006–4094 (requires VTP v3 or transparent mode on most platforms). Total possible: 4094 (12-bit VLAN ID field in 802.1Q: 2^12 = 4096, minus reserved values 0 and 4095).
hints: Normal range: 1-1005; extended: 1006-4094
802.1Q has 12-bit VLAN field = 4096 values (0 and 4095 reserved)

questions.question: EtherChannel is configured. "show etherchannel summary" shows Po1 as "SU". What does "SU" indicate?
options: S=Layer 2 (switched), U=In-use (the bundle is up and operating)
questions.explanation: EtherChannel summary flags: S = Layer 2 EtherChannel (switched). U = in-use (bundle is active and forwarding). Common combinations: SU = L2 in-use. RU = Layer 3 (routed) in-use. D = down. P = bundled in port-channel. I = stand-alone. H = hot-standby (LACP only).
hints: S = Layer 2 EtherChannel
U = in-use/active

questions.question: CDP is a security risk because it reveals device information to any connected device. How do you disable it on a single interface?
options: On the interface: "no cdp enable" — disables CDP on that interface only while keeping it global
CDP cannot be disabled per interface
Use "cdp filter" to block specific information
questions.explanation: "no cdp run" disables CDP globally on the device. "no cdp enable" on an interface disables CDP on just that interface. Best practice: disable CDP on external-facing ports (internet, DMZ, provider connections) while keeping it on internal inter-device links for troubleshooting value.
hints: "no cdp enable" (interface command) = per-interface disable
"no cdp run" = global disable

questions.question: What is the purpose of "spanning-tree loopguard default"?
options: Prevents BPDU loops on access ports
Prevents a port that stops receiving BPDUs from transitioning to forwarding (loop guard puts it in loop-inconsistent state instead of designated/forwarding)
Enables loop detection on all physical ports regardless of STP state
Reduces STP convergence time by bypassing loop detection
questions.explanation: Loop Guard prevents unidirectional link failures from causing STP loops. Normally if BPDUs stop arriving on a blocking port, STP transitions it to designated/forwarding (thinking the neighbor disappeared) — a loop occurs if the link is still physically up. Loop Guard instead places the port in "loop-inconsistent" (blocking) state. Auto-recovers when BPDUs resume.
hints: Loop Guard: port stops receiving BPDUs → loop-inconsistent (not forwarding)
Prevents unidirectional link failure from creating STP loops

questions.question: What command sets a switch port as an access port and assigns it to VLAN 50?
options: In interface config: "switchport mode access" then "switchport access vlan 50"
In global config: "interface vlan 50" then "switchport access"
questions.explanation: Two required commands in interface configuration mode: (1) "switchport mode access" — sets the port to permanent access mode (no DTP negotiation). (2) "switchport access vlan 50" — assigns the port to VLAN 50. Order matters: "mode access" first prevents unintended trunk negotiation.
hints: "switchport mode access" = access mode (no DTP)
"switchport access vlan 50" = assign to VLAN 50

questions.question: PAgP modes: auto and desirable. What combination forms an EtherChannel?
options: desirable+desirable, or desirable+auto; auto+auto does NOT form a bundle
questions.explanation: PAgP negotiation: "desirable" actively sends PAgP PDUs. "auto" passively waits. For an EtherChannel: desirable+desirable = yes (both active). desirable+auto = yes (desirable initiates, auto responds). auto+auto = NO (neither sends PDUs). Compare LACP: active+active=yes, active+passive=yes, passive+passive=NO.
hints: PAgP desirable = active; auto = passive
auto+auto = no bundle (neither initiates)

questions.question: How does a switch handle a frame destined for a MAC address that IS in its CAM table?
options: Floods it to verify the destination is still reachable
Forwards it unicast to the single port associated with that MAC address
Sends it to the CPU for routing
Broadcasts it then discards redundant copies
questions.explanation: Known unicast forwarding: the switch looks up the destination MAC in the CAM (content-addressable memory / MAC table). If found, the frame is forwarded out ONLY the associated port (unicast — not flooded). If not found, it is flooded. This is the fundamental switching operation.
hints: Known unicast = single port forwarding (no flood)
Unknown unicast = flooded within VLAN

questions.question: What does "show mac address-table aging-time" display and what is the default?
options: The maximum number of MACs; default 1024
The time in seconds after which an inactive MAC entry is flushed; default 300 seconds (5 minutes)
The time between BPDU transmissions; default 2 seconds
The MAC learning rate limit; default 100 per second
questions.explanation: MAC address table aging: each entry has a timer reset when traffic is seen from that MAC. If no traffic for the aging time (default 300s), the entry is deleted. This prevents the table from filling with stale entries from disconnected devices. During STP topology changes, aging is shortened to forward_delay (15s) to flush stale entries faster.
hints: MAC aging default = 300 seconds (5 minutes)
Entry deleted if no traffic seen for aging-time duration

questions.question: What is the function of the "spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary" command?
options: Declares the switch as root with priority 0
Sets the switch priority to 24576 (or lower than current root) to ensure it becomes root for VLAN 10
Manually assigns a static root bridge ignoring BID
Forces all switches to recalculate their root port
questions.explanation: "spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary" macro: Cisco IOS checks the current root bridge priority and sets this switch's priority to either 24576 (if the current root has default priority 32768) or current_root_priority - 4096 (to be lower). It does NOT simply set priority to 0. For an explicit value, use "spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096".
hints: "root primary" sets priority to 24576 or lower than current root
Explicit: "spanning-tree vlan N priority 4096" for precise control

questions.question: Two switches, both Cisco, connected via a trunk. Native VLAN mismatch: SW1=VLAN 1, SW2=VLAN 10. What error appears in syslog?
options: CDP: Native VLAN mismatch discovered on port
%CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH: Native VLAN mismatch discovered on Gi0/1
questions.explanation: Cisco CDP detects native VLAN mismatches on trunk links and generates a syslog message: %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH. CDP compares the native VLAN advertised by each side. This is a warning (severity 4) — the trunk continues to operate but VLAN hopping is possible and untagged traffic is misassigned.
hints: CDP detects native VLAN mismatches and logs %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH
The trunk still operates but untagged traffic is misassigned

questions.question: Which STP timer controls how long a port stays in listening and learning states?
options: Forward delay timer (default 15s per state = 30s total listening+learning)
questions.explanation: STP timers: Hello (2s) = interval between BPDUs sent by root. Max-age (20s) = how long a switch stores a BPDU before considering a neighbor down. Forward delay (15s) = duration of both listening state and learning state. Total transition from blocking to forwarding (802.1D): max-age + 2×forward_delay = 20+30 = 50s in the worst case.
hints: Forward delay = 15s per state (listening + learning = 30s)
Total worst-case convergence: max-age (20s) + 2×forward_delay (30s) = 50s

questions.question: A port channel (EtherChannel) interface is configured with an IP address. What is the purpose?
options: EtherChannel cannot have an IP address
A routed port-channel (Layer 3 EtherChannel) — the logical interface carries routed traffic; physical member ports have "no switchport" and no individual IPs
It enables SVI routing for the EtherChannel VLAN
The IP is used for LACP management only
questions.explanation: Layer 3 EtherChannel: configure "no switchport" on each physical member port (removes L2 switching), then configure an IP address on the Port-channel interface. The physical members carry the aggregated L3 routed traffic. No VLANs or 802.1Q needed. Use for high-bandwidth Layer 3 links between routers or multilayer switches.
hints: L3 EtherChannel: "no switchport" on members + IP on port-channel interface
L2 EtherChannel: no "no switchport" needed; can be trunk or access

questions.question: What does "vlan dot1q tag native" global command do on a Cisco switch?
options: Forces the switch to always use VLAN 1 as native
Causes the switch to tag native VLAN traffic with 802.1Q instead of sending it untagged; used for consistency and security
Disables 802.1Q and forces ISL on all trunks
Enables double-tagging VLAN hopping for testing
questions.explanation: "vlan dot1q tag native" causes the switch to send native VLAN traffic WITH an 802.1Q tag (explicitly tagged with the native VLAN ID). This ensures both ends process it correctly and prevents VLAN hopping attacks based on double-tagging. Required on some newer platforms by default. Both sides must have this enabled or disabled consistently.
hints: Tags native VLAN traffic instead of sending it untagged
Prevents double-tagging VLAN hopping; both sides must match

questions.question: A port in "show spanning-tree vlan 20" shows role=Desg, State=FWD. What does this mean?
options: The port is designated (forwarding toward the non-root segment) and actively forwarding data
The port is in the process of becoming root
The port failed STP election and is forwarding anyway
questions.explanation: STP port roles: Root (best path toward root), Designated (best path away from root on a segment), Alternate/Backup (blocked). States: BLK=Blocking, LIS=Listening, LRN=Learning, FWD=Forwarding. Desg+FWD = this port is the designated port for its segment and actively forwarding frames — the normal operating state for designated ports.
hints: Desg = Designated port (best path away from root on this segment)
FWD = Forwarding (actively passing data frames)

questions.question: What is the purpose of PVLAN (Private VLAN) and name its port types?
options: Same as standard VLAN — PVLAN adds no new features
PVLAN isolates hosts within the same subnet from each other. Port types: Promiscuous (talks to all), Isolated (only talks to promiscuous), Community (talks to same-community + promiscuous)
PVLAN creates a separate routing domain per VLAN
PVLAN is used for multi-tenant firewalls only
questions.explanation: Private VLAN (PVLAN) allows host isolation within the same IP subnet. Use case: hotel/ISP where customers share a subnet but should not communicate directly. Port types: Promiscuous (connected to router/gateway — can talk to all). Isolated (most restricted — only communicates with promiscuous). Community (communicates with same-community members + promiscuous).
hints: PVLAN = intra-subnet isolation
Promiscuous=gateway; Isolated=no peer comm; Community=same group only

questions.question: UDLD (Unidirectional Link Detection) is enabled. A fiber cut makes the link unidirectional (TX works, RX fails). What does UDLD do?
options: Detects the unidirectional failure and err-disables the port; prevents STP loops caused by a port that can send but not receive BPDUs
Sends a syslog warning but keeps the port up
questions.explanation: UDLD monitors bidirectional link integrity by sending periodic UDLD frames. If echoed frames are not received back (unidirectional failure), UDLD err-disables the port. This prevents STP loops: a port that can send but not receive BPDUs looks up in STP (no BPDUs received → transitions to forwarding) while actually not hearing the topology.
hints: UDLD: detects one-way link failures (TX ok, RX broken)
Err-disables the port to prevent STP loops from unidirectional links

questions.question: A switch port configured with `switchport trunk native vlan 99` is connected to another switch with `switchport trunk native vlan 1`. What is the impact?
options: Native VLAN mismatch — frames tagged VLAN 1 on one side are treated as native (untagged) on the other, causing traffic to leak across VLANs
No impact; switches negotiate the native VLAN automatically via DTP
Traffic is load-balanced between the two native VLANs
The trunk is shut down by STP because of the mismatch
questions.explanation: Native VLAN mismatch means untagged frames sent by one switch are assigned to a different VLAN by the other. This can cause serious security issues (VLAN hopping). CDP warns about native VLAN mismatches.
hints: Native VLAN carries untagged traffic — mismatch = wrong VLAN assignment

questions.question: You have a trunk link and need VLAN 10 traffic to traverse it but VLAN 20 should be blocked. Which command achieves this?
questions.explanation: `switchport trunk allowed vlan 10` sets the allowed VLAN list to ONLY VLAN 10 on that trunk. `allowed vlan except` keeps existing list minus specified VLANs.
hints: 'allowed vlan X' replaces the list; 'allowed vlan add X' appends

questions.question: STP elects a root bridge. Which tiebreaker is used when two switches have the same bridge priority?
questions.explanation: Bridge ID = priority (32768 default) + MAC address. If priorities are equal, the switch with the LOWEST MAC address wins root bridge election.
hints: Lower Bridge ID wins; MAC is the tiebreaker

questions.question: A port in Rapid PVST+ goes through which states? (Select the correct sequence)
questions.explanation: RSTP (802.1w) consolidates states into Discarding, Learning, Forwarding. Traditional STP uses Blocking, Listening, Learning, Forwarding. Rapid PVST+ uses RSTP states.
hints: RSTP = 3 states: Discarding, Learning, Forwarding

questions.question: What distinguishes a Designated Port from a Root Port in STP?
options: Root Port is the best port toward the root bridge on a non-root switch; Designated Port is the best port on each network segment toward the root
Root Port is on the root bridge; Designated Port is on non-root switches
Both port types forward traffic; only the names differ by vendor
Designated Port is always in blocking state; Root Port forwards
questions.explanation: Every non-root switch has exactly one Root Port (best path toward root). Each segment has exactly one Designated Port (the port on the segment closest to root, which forwards).
hints: Root Port: toward root. Designated Port: on each segment, away from root.

questions.question: Which VTP mode allows a switch to create, modify, and delete VLANs but does NOT propagate VTP advertisements?
questions.explanation: VTP Transparent switches can create/modify/delete local VLANs but don't originate or accept VTP updates. They do forward VTP advertisements to other switches.
hints: Transparent = local VLANs only; forwards but ignores VTP ads

questions.question: An access port is configured with `switchport port-security maximum 2`. A third device connects. What happens by default?
options: The port is placed into err-disabled state (shutdown violation mode)
The third device's MAC is added, replacing the oldest entry
The port sends a trap but continues forwarding
The port drops traffic from the third device but stays up
questions.explanation: Default violation mode is shutdown — the port immediately goes to err-disabled. It must be manually re-enabled (or error-recovery configured) after a violation.
hints: Default violation mode = shutdown → err-disabled

questions.question: What is the purpose of BPDU Guard?
options: Immediately err-disables a PortFast-enabled port if a BPDU is received, preventing unauthorized switches from connecting
Blocks BPDUs from being sent on trunk ports to prevent topology changes
Prevents the root bridge from sending BPDUs to edge devices
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard protects edge (PortFast) ports. If any BPDU arrives on those ports (indicating a switch was connected), the port is err-disabled immediately.
hints: BPDU Guard = enforcement for access/PortFast ports

questions.question: You need to configure Layer 3 EtherChannel between two switches. Which interface must you configure the IP address on?
questions.explanation: For L3 EtherChannel, assign the IP to the Port-channel (logical) interface, not the physical member links. Members are added with `channel-group` commands.
hints: Port-channel is the logical bundle interface

questions.question: Which LACP mode combination results in an EtherChannel forming?
questions.explanation: LACP Active=sends LACP PDUs. Passive=responds only. Active-Active or Active-Passive form a channel. Passive-Passive = no channel (neither initiates). 'On' forces the channel without LACP negotiation and will NOT work with LACP modes.
hints: At least one side must be Active for LACP to negotiate

questions.question: A switch port is in the 'err-disabled' state. Which command re-enables it without reloading the switch?
questions.explanation: Enter the interface and issue `shutdown` followed by `no shutdown` to manually clear err-disabled. Alternatively configure `errdisable recovery cause` for automatic recovery.
hints: Manual reset: shutdown + no shutdown on the interface

questions.question: What does the `spanning-tree portfast` command do when applied to a switch port?
options: The port transitions directly from discarding to forwarding, skipping learning, without waiting for STP convergence
The port becomes the root port permanently
The port sends BPDUs at an accelerated rate
The port is excluded from STP entirely
questions.explanation: PortFast makes the port go directly to forwarding state (skipping listening/learning or discarding/learning), used for end-device ports to avoid 30-second STP delay.
hints: PortFast is for access ports connected to end devices, not switches

questions.question: A trunk port shows VLAN 30 as 'not allowed on trunk' in `show interfaces trunk`. What is the most likely cause?
options: VLAN 30 was removed from the allowed VLAN list with `switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 30`
VLAN 30 does not exist in the VLAN database
questions.explanation: 'Not allowed on trunk' means the VLAN was explicitly removed from the trunk's allowed VLAN list. If the VLAN simply didn't exist, it would show as 'not active.'
hints: 'Not allowed' vs 'not active' — these are distinct states in show interfaces trunk

questions.question: Which standard defines Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP)?
questions.explanation: 802.1w = RSTP. 802.1D = original STP. 802.1s = MSTP (Multiple Spanning Tree). 802.1Q = VLAN trunking.
hints: w = rapid, D = original, s = multiple

questions.question: What is the function of the VTP revision number?
options: Higher revision number = more recent database; switches update their VLAN database when they receive a VTP ad with a higher revision than their own
Lower revision number = more trusted; switches accept ads from switches with lower revision
Revision number tracks the number of VTP client switches
Revision number identifies the VTP domain name uniquely
questions.explanation: VTP uses revision numbers to determine if an update is newer. A switch with a higher revision overwrites lower-revision switches — a classic VTP gotcha when adding a misconfigured switch.
hints: High revision number = newest database; can wipe VLANs if you add a switch carelessly

questions.question: Which command verifies that an EtherChannel is operational and shows the member interfaces?
questions.explanation: `show etherchannel summary` displays the port-channel groups, protocol, and member port states with flags (P=in-bundle, D=down, etc.).
hints: Look for SU (L3 in use) or RU (L2) flags

questions.question: A switch has four switches in the network and must elect a root bridge for VLAN 10. Switch A has priority 24576, Switch B 32768, Switch C 32768, Switch D 4096. Which becomes root?
questions.explanation: Root bridge election: lowest Bridge ID wins. Bridge ID = priority + VLAN + MAC. Switch D has priority 4096 — the lowest among all four switches, so it wins regardless of MAC.
hints: Lowest priority wins; MAC only breaks ties among equal priorities

questions.question: What is a VLAN hopping attack, and which two techniques are used? (Select the best answer)
options: Attacker gains access to traffic on a different VLAN using switch spoofing or double tagging
Attacker overflows the MAC table to force unknown unicast flooding
Attacker injects rogue DHCP offers to intercept traffic
Attacker hijacks VTP server to delete VLANs
questions.explanation: VLAN hopping: (1) Switch spoofing — attacker negotiates a trunk link to receive all VLAN traffic. (2) Double tagging — attacker adds two 802.1Q headers; outer stripped by first switch, inner carries attacker to victim VLAN.
hints: Two methods: trunk negotiation and double-tagging

questions.question: Which STP port type does NOT forward traffic but receives BPDUs and can immediately transition to forwarding if the Designated or Root port fails?
questions.explanation: RSTP Alternate port: in discarding state, provides a backup path to the root. Immediately transitions to forwarding if the current Root Port fails (no 30-second wait).
hints: Alternate = upstream backup; Backup = same-segment backup

questions.question: Which PAgP mode combination will form an EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: PAgP: Desirable=actively negotiates, Auto=responds only. Desirable-Desirable or Desirable-Auto form channels. Auto-Auto = no channel. 'On' is not a PAgP mode — it's mode 'on' which bypasses negotiation.
hints: PAgP: at least one side must be Desirable

questions.question: When `show vlan brief` shows VLAN 50 as active but it does NOT appear in `show interfaces trunk` on the uplink, what is the problem?
options: VLAN 50 was removed from the trunk's allowed VLAN list
VLAN 50 is not in the VLAN database
The trunk is in access mode
VLAN 50 is the management VLAN and is always excluded from trunks
questions.explanation: If the VLAN exists (`show vlan brief` shows active) but doesn't appear on the trunk, it was removed from the trunk allowed list with `switchport trunk allowed vlan remove`.
hints: VLAN active but not on trunk = manually removed from allowed list

questions.question: Sticky MAC learning is enabled on a port. What happens to the learned MAC addresses when the switch reloads if the running config was NOT saved?
options: The sticky MACs are lost; the port relearns them after reload
The sticky MACs are permanent — they survive reloads without saving
The port enters err-disabled state at reload
The switch automatically saves sticky MACs to startup config every 5 minutes
questions.explanation: Sticky MACs appear in the running config. If you don't `write memory`, they are lost on reload. After reload the port relearns new sticky MACs.
hints: Always `wr mem` after sticky learning if you want persistence

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Spanning Tree BPDU Root Path Cost?
options: The cumulative cost of all links from a switch to the root bridge, used to elect Root Ports and Designated Ports
The cost of the local port only, used to determine whether to block or forward
The hop count from the root bridge to the current switch
The administrative cost configured manually to influence VLAN traffic
questions.explanation: Root Path Cost is the sum of port costs along the path from a switch to the root bridge. Switches compare root path costs to elect Root Ports (per switch) and Designated Ports (per segment).
hints: Cumulative cost, not just one hop

questions.question: You configure `switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q` but the command is rejected. What is the likely reason?
options: The switch only supports 802.1Q and the command is unnecessary; dot1q is the only option
The interface is already in access mode
The switch is in client mode and cannot change trunk encapsulation
questions.explanation: On modern Catalyst switches, ISL is not supported, so the only option is 802.1Q (dot1q). The `encapsulation` command is not needed or available on switches that only support dot1q.
hints: ISL is legacy Cisco; most modern switches are dot1q only

questions.question: Which command shows whether a port is currently operating as a trunk and which VLANs are forwarding?
questions.explanation: `show interfaces trunk` lists all trunk ports with their mode, native VLAN, allowed VLANs, and active/forwarding VLANs. `show vlan brief` shows VLAN-to-port membership for access ports.
hints: Trunk status = show interfaces trunk

questions.question: What is the purpose of the STP Topology Change Notification (TCN) BPDU?
options: A non-root switch sends a TCN toward the root to notify it of a port state change; the root then sets the TC flag in BPDUs, causing all switches to age MAC tables faster
The root bridge uses TCNs to recalculate the spanning tree topology
TCNs are used only when a switch loses its uplink to the core
TCNs carry the new root bridge ID after an election
questions.explanation: When a port transitions (up or down), the switch sends a TCN BPDU upstream toward the root. The root acknowledges and sets the TC (Topology Change) bit in its BPDUs for the Forward Delay period, causing switches to flush MAC tables faster (from 300s to Forward Delay).
hints: TCN causes short MAC table aging across the topology

questions.question: Which STP port cost is assigned to a 10 Gbps link using the long path cost method?
questions.explanation: Long (IEEE 802.1t) STP path costs: 10 Mbps=2000000, 100 Mbps=200000, 1 Gbps=20000, 10 Gbps=2000, but simplified: 10Gbps=2 in long method. Short method: 10Gbps=2 also. 1Gbps short=4. 100Mbps short=19.
hints: Short path cost: 10Gbps=2, 1Gbps=4, 100Mbps=19, 10Mbps=100

questions.question: A port configured with `spanning-tree guard root` receives a superior BPDU. What happens?
options: The port is placed in root-inconsistent state (effectively blocking) to prevent an unauthorized root bridge
The port transitions to the root port role
The switch demotes its own root bridge priority
The port forwards the superior BPDU to the current root bridge
questions.explanation: Root Guard prevents a connected device from becoming the root bridge. If a superior BPDU arrives on a root-guard port, the port enters root-inconsistent (blocking) state. It auto-recovers when superior BPDUs stop.
hints: Root Guard = enforce topology at the edge; port blocks if superior BPDU received

questions.question: A trunk is configured with `switchport trunk native vlan 10`. What tag behavior applies to frames sent on VLAN 10?
options: VLAN 10 frames are sent untagged on this trunk
VLAN 10 frames are tagged with VLAN 10 like all other VLANs
VLAN 10 frames are dropped if the peer also uses a native VLAN
Native VLAN frames always use the PVST+ tag
questions.explanation: The native VLAN is transmitted UNTAGGED on 802.1Q trunk links. All other VLANs are tagged with their VLAN ID.
hints: Native VLAN = untagged on trunk

questions.question: What is the extended system ID (sys-id-ext) in PVST+ bridge priority?
options: The VLAN ID is added to the priority field, making bridge priorities VLAN-specific
An extended MAC address used when the switch has more than 4096 interfaces
A secondary priority used when two switches share the same MAC address
The domain name used in VTP advertisements
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1t extended system ID: the VLAN ID is ORed into the 16-bit Bridge ID priority field (the lower 12 bits). So bridge priority must be in multiples of 4096, and the actual bridge ID includes the VLAN.
hints: Priority increments = 4096 because bottom 12 bits = VLAN ID

questions.question: A switch is configured: `switchport mode dynamic desirable`. The connected switch has `switchport mode access`. What is the resulting link type?
options: Access link — access mode overrides dynamic negotiation
Trunk link — desirable actively negotiates a trunk
questions.explanation: Dynamic desirable will try to negotiate a trunk with DTP, but access mode does not participate in DTP and will not form a trunk. The result is an access link.
hints: Access mode statically sets the port; it doesn't negotiate

questions.question: Which EtherChannel hashing algorithm is best for traffic between many different source-destination IP pairs?
questions.explanation: Source-destination IP hashing distributes traffic based on both endpoints, providing better load distribution across EtherChannel members when many unique IP pairs are communicating.
hints: More entropy = better load balancing; src-dst > src-only or dst-only

questions.question: What does the `show mac address-table aging-time` output represent?
options: The number of seconds before a dynamically learned MAC entry is removed if no frames are received
The time since the switch last refreshed the MAC table
The maximum number of MAC addresses the table can hold
The interval between MAC table synchronizations in a stack
questions.explanation: Aging time is the inactivity timer. If a MAC address hasn't been seen on a port for this duration (default 300s), the entry is removed to keep the table fresh.
hints: Aging time = idle timeout for dynamic MAC entries

questions.question: Which condition causes a Cisco switch port to immediately enter err-disabled when `violation restrict` is configured for port security?
options: Violation restrict does NOT err-disable — it drops frames from unknown MACs and increments a counter
It enters err-disabled when more than 1 violation occurs
Restrict mode err-disables after 3 violations in 10 seconds
Restrict is identical to shutdown mode
questions.explanation: Port security violation modes: shutdown=err-disable immediately, restrict=drop+log+counter but port stays up, protect=drop silently. Restrict never err-disables the port.
hints: shutdown=err-disable; restrict=drop+count; protect=drop silently

questions.question: A PVST+ network has 10 VLANs. How many STP instances does the network run?
options: 1 — PVST+ uses a single shared instance
2 — one for odd VLANs, one for even
It depends on the number of switches
questions.explanation: Per-VLAN Spanning Tree Plus (PVST+) runs a separate STP instance for each VLAN, enabling per-VLAN root bridge placement and load balancing across trunks.
hints: PVST+ = separate STP per VLAN; MSTP = multiple VLANs per instance

questions.question: Which command configures a switch as the preferred root bridge for VLAN 20?
questions.explanation: `spanning-tree vlan 20 root primary` sets the priority to 24576 (or lower if needed to beat current root). Alternatively, manually set a low priority like 4096 with `spanning-tree vlan 20 priority 4096`.
hints: 'root primary' macro sets priority low automatically

questions.question: What does the `switchport nonegotiate` command do on a trunk port?
options: Disables DTP on the port so it does not send or respond to DTP frames
Removes all VLANs from the trunk
Converts the port to access mode silently
Enables trunk negotiation with all connected devices
questions.explanation: `switchport nonegotiate` disables DTP entirely on that port. Used to prevent unauthorized DTP negotiation (security best practice on trunk ports connected to non-Cisco devices).
hints: nonegotiate = no DTP frames sent or processed

questions.question: In a switch stack, which switch becomes the stack master?
options: The switch with the highest stack member priority (1–15); ties broken by MAC address
The switch with the lowest priority
The switch with the most uplink connections
questions.explanation: Stack master election: highest priority (default 1; configured 1-15) wins. Ties broken by longest uptime, then by MAC address. Configured priority takes precedence.
hints: Highest priority wins stack master election

questions.question: What is the risk of setting VTP mode to Server with a default domain name and high revision number in a new network deployment?
options: The switch can overwrite the entire VLAN database of all VTP client switches if it has a higher revision number
The switch cannot join the network until the domain name is set
VTP server mode prevents creating new VLANs
The switch automatically becomes root bridge
questions.explanation: Classic VTP gotcha: if a switch with a high revision number joins the domain, all VTP clients accept its VLAN database, potentially wiping existing VLANs. Reset revision to 0 by changing VTP mode or domain name before adding to production.
hints: High revision = overwrites everyone — always reset revision before adding switches

questions.question: Which spanning tree feature prevents a port from forwarding traffic if it does not receive BPDUs for a configurable time, protecting against unidirectional link failures?
questions.explanation: Loop Guard prevents a non-designated port from transitioning to forwarding when BPDUs stop arriving (e.g., unidirectional link failure). Without it, the port would mistakenly become a Designated Port and create a loop.
hints: Loop Guard = protects against BPDU loss on non-designated ports

questions.question: An access port carries VLAN 50. A host sends a frame tagged with VLAN 50. What does the switch do?
options: The switch drops the frame because access ports do not accept tagged frames
The switch forwards the frame normally, stripping the tag
The switch places it on the trunk for VLAN 50
The switch re-tags it with the native VLAN
questions.explanation: Access ports expect UNTAGGED frames. By default, a tagged frame arriving on an access port is typically dropped. Cisco CDP/LLDP use a native VLAN tag exception, but normal data tagged frames on access ports are dropped.
hints: Access ports = untagged; tagged frames on access ports = dropped

questions.question: What is the function of the Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP)?
options: Discovers directly connected Cisco devices and shares device type, IOS version, IP addresses, and interface info
Discovers Layer 3 routes from neighboring routers
Monitors port states for STP convergence
questions.explanation: CDP is a Cisco proprietary L2 protocol that multicasts device information to directly connected neighbors. Used for topology discovery and troubleshooting. Disable on untrusted ports (security risk).
hints: CDP = Cisco only; LLDP is the open standard equivalent

questions.question: What does `show interfaces gi0/1 switchport` tell you that `show interfaces gi0/1` does not?
options: VLAN membership, trunking mode, and operational trunk/access state
MAC address table entries on that port
questions.explanation: `show interfaces switchport` shows the VLAN configuration (access VLAN, voice VLAN, trunk allowed VLANs, DTP mode, native VLAN). The basic `show interfaces` shows physical layer stats.
hints: switchport keyword adds VLAN/trunk info to the output

questions.question: Which RSTP port role replaces the traditional STP 'Blocking' role and provides a backup to the Designated Port on the same segment?
questions.explanation: RSTP Backup port: provides a redundant (backup) path to the same segment as the Designated Port on the same switch. Both ports connect to the same segment. Alternate provides backup to the Root Port.
hints: Backup = same segment as DP; Alternate = backup to Root Port

questions.question: A switch port is configured as `switchport mode trunk`. The connected device is a PC (not a switch). What is the result?
options: The trunk is formed but the PC likely cannot pass traffic unless it has 802.1Q VLAN support
The port automatically falls back to access mode
The switch drops all traffic from the PC
The PC receives all VLAN traffic, creating a security risk
questions.explanation: A statically configured trunk port sends and expects 802.1Q tagged frames. A regular PC NIC doesn't understand VLAN tags and will likely drop tagged frames or fail to communicate correctly.
hints: Trunk ports expect tagged frames; PCs typically don't handle them

questions.question: What is the RSTP proposal/agreement mechanism and why is it faster than traditional STP?
options: When a port comes up, it proposes to be Designated; if the downstream switch agrees (agreement), both sides synchronize simultaneously — no delay timers needed
RSTP uses a shorter hello interval (1s vs 2s) to detect failures faster
RSTP eliminates the learning state for all ports
RSTP only applies to non-root switches
questions.explanation: RSTP's P/A (Proposal/Agreement) handshake allows rapid transition to forwarding on point-to-point links without waiting for Forward Delay timers. The upstream port proposes; the downstream switch puts all non-edge ports in sync (discarding) and sends an agreement, completing in one exchange.
hints: P/A handshake replaces the 30-second listen+learn timer

questions.question: Which statement is true about a port in RSTP 'discarding' state?
options: It does not forward data frames or learn MAC addresses, but still receives BPDUs
It forwards BPDUs but not data frames
It is equivalent to a shutdown port
It forwards data but not BPDUs
questions.explanation: RSTP Discarding state: port receives BPDUs and participates in RSTP but does not forward data frames and does not learn MAC addresses. It combines the old Blocking and Listening states.
hints: Discarding = no data forwarding, no MAC learning, but BPDUs received

questions.question: A switch has `spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst` configured. What protocol is active?
questions.explanation: Cisco's `rapid-pvst` mode runs Rapid PVST+ — a per-VLAN implementation of IEEE 802.1w (RSTP). `pvst` mode runs classic per-VLAN 802.1D. `mst` mode runs 802.1s.
hints: rapid-pvst = RSTP per VLAN; pvst = classic STP per VLAN; mst = MSTP

questions.question: Two switches are connected with an EtherChannel using LACP. One switch has `channel-group 1 mode passive`, the other has `channel-group 1 mode passive`. What happens?
options: No EtherChannel forms — both passive sides wait for the other to initiate
The channel forms but without LACP negotiation
One switch automatically upgrades to active mode
questions.explanation: LACP passive mode only responds to LACP PDUs; it never initiates. Two passive switches sit silently waiting for the other to send LACP PDUs — no channel forms.
hints: Passive+Passive = no channel; need at least one Active

questions.question: Which Cisco feature auto-recovers err-disabled ports after a configured delay?
questions.explanation: `errdisable recovery cause <cause>` enables automatic recovery for a specific cause (e.g., psecure-violation, bpduguard). `errdisable recovery interval <seconds>` sets the delay before recovery attempt.
hints: errdisable recovery cause = automatic port recovery; default interval = 300s

questions.question: What is the purpose of LLDP (Link Layer Discovery Protocol)?
options: An open standard (IEEE 802.1AB) equivalent to CDP that discovers directly connected device capabilities regardless of vendor
A Cisco proprietary protocol for VLAN discovery
A protocol for STP topology advertisement
questions.explanation: LLDP (IEEE 802.1AB) is the vendor-neutral alternative to Cisco CDP. It discovers directly connected neighbors and shares device type, port ID, and capabilities. Essential for multi-vendor environments.
hints: LLDP = vendor-neutral CDP; 802.1AB; works with non-Cisco devices

questions.question: A switch interface shows input errors and late collisions. What is the most likely cause?
options: Duplex mismatch — one side is full-duplex, the other is half-duplex
Speed mismatch between the two interfaces
questions.explanation: Duplex mismatch: the full-duplex side transmits freely while the half-duplex side uses CSMA/CD, causing collisions. Late collisions (occurring after 64 bytes) are a classic duplex mismatch symptom.
hints: Late collisions + errors = duplex mismatch; check both sides

questions.question: What does `show spanning-tree vlan 10` display?
options: Root bridge ID, root path cost, local bridge ID, and port roles/states for VLAN 10
The MAC address table for VLAN 10
questions.explanation: `show spanning-tree vlan 10` shows the root bridge information (priority, MAC, cost), the local switch bridge ID, and a table of all VLAN 10 ports with their STP role (Root/Desg/Altn) and state (FWD/BLK/LRN).
hints: Output includes: root ID, bridge ID, port roles, and states per VLAN

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes Voice VLANs (auxiliary VLANs) on a Cisco access port?
options: An access port can simultaneously carry one data VLAN (untagged) and one voice VLAN (802.1Q tagged for the phone), allowing a PC and IP phone to share a single port
Voice VLANs require a dedicated trunk port
Voice VLANs are only supported on FastEthernet interfaces
An access port must be converted to a trunk to support a voice VLAN
questions.explanation: Cisco IP phones can be connected to access ports configured with `switchport voice vlan <id>`. The port sends the data VLAN untagged and the voice VLAN tagged (CDP informs the phone). No full trunk needed.
hints: switchport voice vlan = mini-trunk: data VLAN untagged, voice VLAN tagged

questions.question: What does `show interfaces gi0/1` showing 'FastEthernet1/0 is up, line protocol is down' indicate?
options: Physical connection exists (up) but the data link layer cannot establish (protocol down) — likely a keepalive or encapsulation mismatch
Both physical and logical layers are healthy
The interface is administratively shut down
questions.explanation: Up/down: physical layer active (carrier detected) but L2 cannot establish. On serial links this often means clocking/keepalive issues. On Ethernet it could mean encapsulation mismatch or the connected device isn't sending L2 keepalives.
hints: Up/down = physical OK, data link failed; down/down = physical failed; admin down = shutdown

questions.question: Which command shows the current VTP status, domain name, revision number, and mode?
questions.explanation: `show vtp status` displays: VTP version, domain name, revision number, operating mode (server/client/transparent), and number of VLANs.
hints: show vtp status = all VTP parameters in one command

questions.question: A port is configured with `spanning-tree bpdufilter enable`. What is the effect?
options: The port stops sending and receiving BPDUs, effectively removing it from STP participation — creates loop risk
The port filters malformed BPDUs only
The port stops sending BPDUs but still receives them
BPDUs are logged but not processed
questions.explanation: BPDU Filter stops BPDUs being sent and received on that port. Unlike BPDU Guard (which err-disables on receipt), BPDU Filter simply ignores BPDUs. This removes the port from STP and risks loops if connected to another switch.
hints: BPDU Filter = ignores BPDUs; BPDU Guard = err-disable on receipt

questions.question: Which STP port cost is associated with a 1 Gbps link using the short (default) path cost method?
questions.explanation: Default (short) STP port costs: 10 Mbps=100, 100 Mbps=19, 1 Gbps=4, 10 Gbps=2.
hints: Short path costs: 10G=2, 1G=4, 100M=19, 10M=100

questions.question: An EtherChannel member port goes down. What happens to the EtherChannel?
options: The remaining ports continue to forward traffic; the bundle stays up with reduced bandwidth
The entire EtherChannel fails and all traffic stops
STP immediately blocks the remaining ports
The port-channel interface is removed from the routing table
questions.explanation: EtherChannel degrades gracefully. When a member link fails, traffic is redistributed across remaining links. The Port-channel interface stays up as long as at least one member link is active.
hints: EtherChannel is resilient — one link down = redistribute traffic, bundle stays up

questions.question: What is the maximum number of active links in an 802.3ad (LACP) EtherChannel?
options: 8 active links (up to 8 standby links allowed)
No limit — all configured links are active
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.3ad LACP supports up to 8 active links and 8 standby links (for a total of 16 configured). Standby links activate when an active link fails.
hints: LACP: 8 active + 8 standby = 16 configured max

questions.question: Which inter-VLAN routing method uses a single router interface with sub-interfaces for each VLAN?
questions.explanation: Router-on-a-Stick: single physical link carries 802.1Q tagged traffic for multiple VLANs. The router has one sub-interface per VLAN (e.g., gi0/0.10, gi0/0.20), each with encapsulation dot1q and IP address.
hints: ROAS = one physical link, multiple sub-interfaces, 802.1Q tagging

questions.question: What is a potential issue when deploying ROAS (Router-on-a-Stick) in a high-traffic environment?
options: The single physical link becomes a bandwidth bottleneck as all inter-VLAN traffic traverses it in both directions
ROAS does not support more than 4 VLANs
Sub-interfaces cannot have separate IP addresses
ROAS requires OSPF to route between VLANs
questions.explanation: All inter-VLAN traffic passes through the single router link twice (in + out). This creates a bandwidth bottleneck at scale. Multi-layer switches (SVIs) solve this by routing at wire speed in silicon.
hints: ROAS bottleneck = one link for ALL inter-VLAN traffic; use L3 switch for scale

questions.question: What distinguishes 802.1s MSTP from 802.1w RSTP?
options: MSTP maps multiple VLANs to a single STP instance, reducing CPU overhead compared to PVST+ which runs one instance per VLAN
MSTP is faster than RSTP in convergence
MSTP only works with fiber uplinks
MSTP eliminates the need for a root bridge
questions.explanation: MSTP (Multiple Spanning Tree) allows grouping of multiple VLANs into MST instances, reducing the number of active STP instances vs. PVST+ (which runs one per VLAN). MSTP still uses RSTP convergence mechanics.
hints: MSTP = fewer instances (VLANs grouped); PVST+ = one per VLAN

questions.question: Which command displays MAC address table entries for a specific VLAN?
questions.explanation: `show mac address-table vlan <id>` filters the MAC address table to show only entries learned on interfaces in that VLAN. Useful for troubleshooting VLAN connectivity.
hints: show mac address-table [dynamic|static|vlan|address|interface]

questions.question: What is the purpose of DHCP Snooping's trusted port designation?
options: Trusted ports allow DHCP server responses (Offers, ACKs) through; untrusted ports only allow client messages (Discover, Request) and drop any DHCP server responses
Trusted ports receive ARP inspection exemptions
Trusted ports bypass port security checks
Trusted ports allow higher-rate DHCP traffic
questions.explanation: DHCP Snooping protects against rogue DHCP servers. Untrusted ports (access/edge) drop any DHCP Offer/ACK coming in (which would indicate a rogue server). Trusted ports (uplinks to real DHCP server) pass all DHCP traffic.
hints: Trusted = uplinks/DHCP server; untrusted = access ports (drops DHCP Offers from edge)

questions.question: A network engineer uses `debug spanning-tree events` and sees 'port Gi0/1 moved to forwarding'. What triggered this?
options: A topology change caused Gi0/1 to transition through STP states and reach the forwarding state
The port was manually configured with `no shutdown`
STP was disabled on that port
The port was connected to a new switch
questions.explanation: STP debug shows port state transitions. Moving to forwarding means the port completed STP convergence (went through blocking/listening/learning in classic STP, or discarding/learning in RSTP).
hints: STP debug: port transitions = topology event completed; port reaching forwarding = converged

questions.question: Which condition can cause an EtherChannel to fail to form even though the physical links are up?
options: Inconsistent configurations across member ports (different allowed VLANs, trunk mode, speed/duplex)
The switch does not support EtherChannel
The port-channel interface is shut down (this only affects traffic, not formation)
questions.explanation: EtherChannel requires all member ports to have identical configuration: speed, duplex, trunk/access mode, allowed VLANs, native VLAN, and spanning-tree settings. Any mismatch prevents the channel from forming.
hints: EtherChannel forms only if all member port configs are identical
