questions.question: A PC cannot reach the default gateway. `show mac address-table` on the switch shows no entry for the gateway's MAC. What is the most likely cause?
options: The gateway's interface is down or it hasn't sent any traffic to trigger MAC learning
The PC has the wrong VLAN configured
Port security is blocking the gateway's MAC
STP has blocked the gateway's port
questions.explanation: Switch learns MACs from INCOMING frames' source MAC. If the gateway's interface is down or no frames have been received from it, there's no MAC table entry. Check interface status first.
hints: MAC table: entries appear only after switch receives a frame from that device

questions.question: What is the purpose of CDP hold time?
options: How long a switch retains a CDP neighbor entry if no new CDP advertisement is received from that neighbor
How often CDP advertisements are sent
The maximum number of CDP neighbors allowed
CDP hold time sets the trunk negotiation timeout
questions.explanation: CDP hold time (default 180 seconds = 3× hello interval of 60 seconds): if a CDP advertisement isn't received from a neighbor within the hold time, the neighbor entry is removed.
hints: CDP hold time = neighbor entry aging timer; default 180s

questions.question: A switch has three ports in VLAN 10 and three in VLAN 20. A broadcast from VLAN 10 is sent. How many ports receive it?
options: Only the 3 VLAN 10 ports — broadcast domains are VLAN-specific
All 6 ports — broadcasts always flood all switch ports
Only the port that sent it plus the uplink trunk
questions.explanation: VLANs define broadcast domains at Layer 2. A broadcast from a VLAN 10 host is confined to VLAN 10 ports only. VLAN 20 ports do not receive it.
hints: VLAN = broadcast domain; broadcasts stay within their VLAN

questions.question: Which switch feature maps VLAN IDs to STP instances, allowing different VLANs to use different spanning tree topologies for load balancing?
options: PVST+ (Per-VLAN Spanning Tree Plus) — different VLANs can have different root bridges
MSTP with one instance per VLAN
Rapid STP with load balancing mode
questions.explanation: PVST+ runs separate STP instances per VLAN. By making different switches root for different VLANs (e.g., SW1 root for VLANs 1-5, SW2 root for VLANs 6-10), traffic is load-balanced across uplink trunks.
hints: PVST+ = per-VLAN root bridge = load balancing across uplinks

questions.question: What is the result of configuring `switchport trunk allowed vlan add 30` vs `switchport trunk allowed vlan 30`?
options: 'add 30' appends VLAN 30 to the existing allowed list; 'allowed vlan 30' REPLACES the entire list with only VLAN 30
'allowed vlan 30' adds VLAN 30; 'add 30' removes all others
questions.explanation: Critical difference: `allowed vlan <id>` REPLACES the allowed list entirely. `allowed vlan add <id>` APPENDS to the existing list. Mistakenly using the replace form can disable all existing VLANs on the trunk.
hints: add = append; no 'add' = replace entire list (dangerous!)

questions.question: Which wireless standard introduced OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) for simultaneous multi-user transmission?
questions.explanation: 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E) introduced OFDMA, allowing the channel to be divided into smaller resource units serving multiple clients simultaneously. Prior standards used OFDM for one user at a time.
hints: Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) = OFDMA for multi-user; previous = OFDM (one at a time)

questions.question: In a wireless network, what is the difference between an autonomous AP and a lightweight AP (LWAP)?
options: Autonomous AP: self-contained, configured independently; Lightweight AP: relies on a WLC for configuration, RF management, and security — just forwards traffic
Lightweight APs are more expensive but provide better coverage
questions.explanation: Autonomous AP: full local configuration, standalone. Lightweight AP (CAPWAP): minimal local config, managed centrally by a Wireless LAN Controller (WLC). All control traffic tunneled to WLC.
hints: Autonomous = standalone; Lightweight = CAPWAP-managed by WLC

questions.question: What does the CAPWAP protocol provide in a lightweight wireless network?
options: Tunnels control and data traffic between lightweight APs and the WLC, allowing centralized management while APs handle only Layer 1/2 functions
Encrypts wireless traffic between client and AP
questions.explanation: CAPWAP (Control and Provisioning of Wireless Access Points): control channel (UDP 5246) carries management traffic; data channel (UDP 5247) optionally tunnels client data to the WLC.
hints: CAPWAP: control=UDP 5246, data=UDP 5247; AP-to-WLC tunnel

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 802.1Q tag inserted in an Ethernet frame?
options: Carries the VLAN ID (12 bits) and priority (3-bit PCP) for frames traversing trunk links between switches
Encrypts the frame for secure VLAN transmission
Identifies the originating switch in a stack
questions.explanation: 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag after the source MAC: TPID (0x8100), 3-bit PCP (priority), DEI bit, and 12-bit VLAN ID. Frames are tagged before traversing trunk links.
hints: 802.1Q tag: TPID + PCP + DEI + VLAN ID (4 bytes total)

questions.question: Which wireless security protocol is vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks against captured 4-way handshakes?
questions.explanation: WPA2-Personal uses PSK authentication. If an attacker captures the 4-way handshake, they can run offline dictionary/brute-force attacks to recover the passphrase. WPA3-SAE (Dragonfly) resists this.
hints: WPA2-PSK captured handshake → offline cracking; WPA3-SAE prevents this

questions.question: What is the purpose of a wireless SSID?
options: The human-readable network name broadcast in Beacon frames that clients use to identify and select a wireless network
The IP subnet associated with the wireless network
The MAC address of the access point
The encryption key used for WPA2
questions.explanation: SSID (Service Set Identifier) is the wireless network name. APs broadcast it in Beacon frames. Clients filter SSIDs to find and connect to their desired network. The SSID is not a security feature.
hints: SSID = wireless network name; hiding it provides minimal security

questions.question: What does `show interfaces gi0/1 counters` show that `show interfaces gi0/1` does not?
options: Separate TX and RX counters with precise octets, unicasts, broadcasts, multicasts, and errors without truncation
questions.explanation: `show interfaces counters` provides detailed per-direction packet and byte counts: input unicasts, multicasts, broadcasts, output same, plus errors — all separately from physical status.
hints: counters = detailed packet statistics; basic show interfaces = status + summary counters

questions.question: A network engineer needs to ensure two specific switches are the primary and secondary root bridges for all VLANs. Which commands achieve this?
options: spanning-tree vlan <id> root primary on SW1; spanning-tree vlan <id> root secondary on SW2
spanning-tree vlan <id> priority 4096 on SW1; spanning-tree vlan <id> priority 8192 on SW2
Both A and B achieve the same result; 'root primary' sets priority to 24576
Both commands are equivalent; root primary/secondary macros set priority to specific values
questions.explanation: Both approaches work. `root primary` sets priority to 24576 (or lower if needed to beat current root). `root secondary` sets to 28672. Alternatively, manually set 4096/8192. Both achieve root placement.
hints: root primary=24576; root secondary=28672; or manually configure lower priorities

questions.question: What is the key difference between the 802.11a and 802.11b wireless standards?
options: 802.11a uses 5 GHz (up to 54 Mbps, more channels, less congested); 802.11b uses 2.4 GHz (up to 11 Mbps, fewer non-overlapping channels, more congestion)
802.11b uses 5 GHz and has 23 non-overlapping channels
They use the same frequency but different modulation
questions.explanation: 802.11a: 5 GHz, up to 54 Mbps, 23 non-overlapping 20 MHz channels (US). 802.11b: 2.4 GHz, up to 11 Mbps, only 3 non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11 in US).
hints: a=5GHz/54Mbps; b=2.4GHz/11Mbps; both released 1999

questions.question: What problem does Spanning Tree solve?
options: Prevents Layer 2 broadcast storms and MAC table instability caused by physical loops in switched networks
Prevents IP address conflicts on VLANs
Prevents duplicate frames from reaching hosts in redundant topologies
questions.explanation: Without STP, physical loops in a switched network would cause broadcast storms (broadcasts loop forever), MAC table instability (frames arrive from multiple ports), and duplicate unicast frames, crashing the network.
hints: STP prevents L2 loops → no broadcast storms → no MAC table thrashing

questions.question: A switch with default STP settings has a 1 Gbps uplink to the root bridge. What is the STP root path cost?
questions.explanation: Default (short) STP port cost for 1 Gbps = 4. Root path cost = sum of port costs along the path to the root. Single 1 Gbps link = cost 4.
hints: 1Gbps short cost=4; 100Mbps=19; 10Mbps=100

questions.question: What wireless roaming event occurs when a client moves between APs in the same SSID but managed by a WLC?
options: Intra-controller roaming — the WLC handles the handoff internally with no traffic interruption
Inter-controller roaming — requires mobility tunnel between WLCs
Both types are the same from the client's perspective
Roaming requires the client to re-associate and re-authenticate completely
questions.explanation: Intra-controller roaming: client moves between two APs managed by the same WLC. The WLC updates the client association internally. Client keeps its IP address — minimal disruption.
hints: Intra-controller = same WLC handles both APs = seamless handoff

questions.question: Which technology allows multiple physical switches to operate as a single logical switch, eliminating STP entirely between the members?
questions.explanation: Cisco StackWise (for Catalyst 9000), VSS (Catalyst 6000), and similar technologies logically merge switches into one. Since there's only one control plane, STP is not needed between stack members.
hints: StackWise/VSS = one logical switch = no STP between members

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 802.11 BSS (Basic Service Set) and what identifies it uniquely?
options: A BSS is a wireless network consisting of one AP and its associated clients; identified by the BSSID (AP's radio MAC address)
BSS identifies the SSID used by the network
BSS is the broadcast domain of the wireless network
BSS is defined by the IP subnet allocated to the wireless LAN
questions.explanation: BSS: single AP + associated clients. BSSID = the AP's radio MAC address (unique identifier). ESS (Extended Service Set) = multiple APs with the same SSID for roaming.
hints: BSS=one AP; BSSID=AP MAC; ESS=multiple APs same SSID for roaming

questions.question: Which 802.11 frame type is used for Beacon, Probe Request/Response, Association, and Authentication?
questions.explanation: 802.11 frame types: Management (beacons, probes, auth, association/reassociation), Control (RTS, CTS, ACK — medium access), Data (actual user data). Action frames are a subtype of Management.
hints: Management: beacons, probes, auth, assoc; Control: RTS/CTS/ACK; Data: payload

questions.question: A WLC shows an AP in 'Discovery' state. What does this mean?
options: The AP has joined the WLC control plane but is discovering its configuration — normal transition state before 'Registered'
The AP is in an error state and needs reboot
Discovery means the AP is operating in local switching mode
The AP is in standalone (autonomous) mode
questions.explanation: CAPWAP join process: AP boots → sends Discovery Request (broadcast/multicast/prior WLC) → receives Discovery Response → sends Join Request → Join Response → AP is Registered and downloads config.
hints: CAPWAP states: Discover → Join → Image Check → Config → Registered

questions.question: What is the purpose of WPA Enterprise vs WPA Personal?
options: WPA Enterprise uses 802.1X with a RADIUS server for per-user authentication; WPA Personal uses a shared passphrase (PSK) for all users
WPA Enterprise encrypts the passphrase; WPA Personal uses plaintext
WPA Enterprise is for home networks; WPA Personal for offices
They are identical except Enterprise supports AES and Personal uses TKIP
questions.explanation: WPA Personal (PSK): single shared passphrase, appropriate for homes/small offices. WPA Enterprise: each user authenticates with unique credentials via 802.1X/EAP to a RADIUS server — required for enterprise security.
hints: Enterprise=802.1X+RADIUS per-user auth; Personal=shared PSK

questions.question: Which 802.11 modulation technique used in 802.11n and 802.11ac allows higher data rates by sending multiple streams simultaneously?
questions.explanation: MIMO uses multiple antennas and spatial streams for parallel data transmission. 802.11n introduced 2×2 MIMO; 802.11ac uses up to 8×8 MU-MIMO. Significantly increases throughput.
hints: MIMO = multiple antennas = multiple parallel streams = higher throughput

questions.question: What command on a Cisco WLC would you use to verify which APs are currently associated and their status?
questions.explanation: `show ap summary` on a Cisco WLC displays all associated APs with name, IP, model, state (Registered), and uptime.
hints: WLC: show ap summary = all APs and their status

questions.question: In a FlexConnect deployment, what is the local switching mode?
options: Traffic is switched locally at the AP without traversing the WLC tunnel — reduces WAN bandwidth for remote sites
All traffic tunnels to the WLC for centralized policy enforcement
FlexConnect forces all traffic to the internet directly
Local switching requires a local WLC at each site
questions.explanation: FlexConnect (formerly HREAP): lightweight APs at remote sites can switch traffic locally (without sending it back to the central WLC), reducing WAN traffic. Management traffic still goes to WLC.
hints: FlexConnect local switching = data plane local; control still via WLC over WAN

questions.question: What is the Wi-Fi frequency band advantage of 5 GHz over 2.4 GHz?
options: More non-overlapping channels and less interference; shorter range trade-off is acceptable in dense environments
5 GHz penetrates walls better than 2.4 GHz
5 GHz is backward compatible with 802.11b devices
5 GHz has lower data rates but more reliable connections
questions.explanation: 5 GHz: 23+ non-overlapping channels, less crowded, supports higher data rates but shorter range due to higher frequency attenuation. 2.4 GHz: only 3 non-overlapping channels (1,6,11), longer range, more interference.
hints: 5GHz: more channels, higher rate, shorter range; 2.4GHz: fewer channels, longer range

questions.question: What does the `switchport port-security mac-address sticky` command do when frames arrive?
options: Dynamically learns MACs and adds them to the running config as static secure addresses — persists across reloads if saved
Adds the MAC address to the MAC address table only, with no security enforcement
Requires all MACs to be pre-configured before the command takes effect
Sticky only works with violation mode restrict
questions.explanation: Sticky MAC: as frames arrive, their source MACs are dynamically learned AND added to the running config as static port-security entries. Save running config to retain them across reboots.
hints: Sticky = dynamic learning + static config entry; save running config to persist

questions.question: A trunk port is configured `switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30`. VLANs 10 and 30 are in `show vlan brief` as active, but VLAN 20 is not in the database. What happens to VLAN 20 traffic on this trunk?
options: VLAN 20 is in the allowed list but not active — its traffic is NOT forwarded because the VLAN doesn't exist in the database
VLAN 20 frames are forwarded transparently as the trunk doesn't check the VLAN database
VLAN 20 is automatically created when added to the trunk allowed list
The trunk goes down because of the missing VLAN
questions.explanation: A VLAN must be: 1) in the allowed list AND 2) active in the VLAN database AND 3) not blocked by STP to appear in the 'active VLANs in spanning tree forwarding state' section of show interfaces trunk.
hints: VLAN forwarding: allowed + active + STP forwarding = all three required

questions.question: A wireless client shows 'Limited Connectivity' after connecting to a WPA2 AP. What is the most likely cause?
options: The client authenticated successfully but DHCP failed — received no IP address
questions.explanation: 'Limited Connectivity' (Windows) or APIPA address means L2 association succeeded but DHCP failed. Causes: DHCP server unreachable, pool exhausted, VLAN misconfiguration, or ACL blocking DHCP.
hints: Limited connectivity = L2 OK, DHCP failed; troubleshoot DHCP path

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 802.11 RTS/CTS mechanism?
options: Reserves the wireless medium before transmitting large frames to prevent collisions, especially useful in hidden node scenarios
RTS/CTS provides encryption for management frames
questions.explanation: RTS/CTS: sender sends RTS, AP responds with CTS. All stations that hear either frame defer transmission for the duration. Solves the hidden node problem where two clients can't hear each other.
hints: RTS/CTS = medium reservation; solves hidden node problem

questions.question: What does 'channel bonding' or 'channel aggregation' do in 802.11n and 802.11ac?
options: Combines two adjacent 20 MHz channels into a 40 MHz (or 80/160 MHz) channel to double or quadruple bandwidth
Allows two APs to share the same channel without interference
Bonds client MACs together for higher throughput
Splits one channel into multiple lower-bandwidth channels
questions.explanation: Channel bonding: 802.11n supports 20/40 MHz; 802.11ac adds 80 MHz and 160 MHz channels. Wider channel = higher throughput (at cost of using more spectrum).
hints: 40 MHz = 2×20 MHz bonded; 80 MHz = 4×20 MHz; wider = more throughput

questions.question: Which command on a Cisco switch enables LLDP globally?
questions.explanation: `lldp run` enables LLDP globally. `lldp transmit` and `lldp receive` enable per interface. `no lldp run` disables globally. CDP and LLDP can run simultaneously.
hints: lldp run = enable LLDP globally; lldp transmit/receive = per-interface

questions.question: What is the maximum cable length for 100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet) over Cat5e UTP?
questions.explanation: 100BASE-TX (and all 10/100/1000 BASE-T Ethernet): 100 meter maximum segment length over UTP Cat5e/Cat6. This includes the cable and any patch panels.
hints: All copper BASE-T Ethernet = 100m max segment

questions.question: A port security violation is set to `protect`. What happens when the maximum MAC count is exceeded?
options: Frames from unknown MACs are silently dropped; the port stays up and no log/trap is generated
A syslog message is generated and the frame is dropped
The excess MAC is added to a 'quarantine' VLAN
questions.explanation: Port security violation modes: protect=drop silently (no log, no trap), restrict=drop+syslog+SNMP trap, shutdown=err-disable the port. Protect is the least disruptive.
hints: Protect=drop+silent; Restrict=drop+log; Shutdown=err-disable

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `spanning-tree vlan <id> priority 4096` command?
options: Lowers the bridge priority to 4096 (below default 32768), increasing the chance this switch wins the root bridge election for that VLAN
Raises the priority to make this switch less likely to be root
Sets the root path cost to 4096
Configures PortFast on all ports for VLAN <id>
questions.explanation: Lower bridge priority = better chance of winning root bridge election. Default = 32768. Valid values must be multiples of 4096: 0, 4096, 8192, ... 61440.
hints: Lower priority = more likely to be root; priority must be multiple of 4096

questions.question: Which 802.11 security improvement in WPA3 replaces the 4-way handshake PSK with a method that prevents offline dictionary attacks?
options: SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals / Dragonfly key exchange)
questions.explanation: WPA3-Personal uses SAE (SAE = Simultaneous Authentication of Equals, based on the Dragonfly handshake). SAE requires real-time interaction for each attempt, making offline dictionary attacks computationally infeasible.
hints: WPA3 SAE = Dragonfly; no offline crack; requires online interaction per attempt

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `switchport access vlan <id>` command?
options: Assigns the port to a specific VLAN when in access mode — all frames sent/received on this port belong to that VLAN
Changes the port from access to trunk mode
Sets the native VLAN on a trunk port
Allows only that VLAN on the trunk
questions.explanation: `switchport access vlan <id>` assigns an access port to a specific VLAN. Must also have `switchport mode access` (or the port must not be in trunk mode). Default VLAN for access ports is VLAN 1.
hints: switchport access vlan = VLAN assignment for access ports

questions.question: Which command shows the STP port role (Root/Designated/Alternate) and state (FWD/BLK) for all VLANs?
questions.explanation: `show spanning-tree` (without VLAN filter) shows STP summary for all VLANs. `show spanning-tree vlan <id>` gives detailed info for a specific VLAN.
hints: show spanning-tree = all VLANs summary; show spanning-tree vlan X = per-VLAN detail

questions.question: What is the 802.11 DIFS (Distributed Interframe Space) used for?
options: A mandatory backoff period before transmitting a data frame in CSMA/CA — ensures the medium is clear before competing for access
DIFS is used only for management frames
DIFS sets the maximum transmission time per frame
DIFS is the WPA key exchange interval
questions.explanation: CSMA/CA: a station waits until the medium is idle for DIFS, then adds a random backoff before transmitting. This reduces simultaneous transmissions.
hints: DIFS = wait time after medium becomes idle before attempting transmission

questions.question: A switch has 24 access ports in VLAN 10 and 2 uplink trunks to the distribution layer. What STP port role will each of the 24 access ports hold?
options: Designated port (forwarding) — access ports are always Designated because no alternative paths exist below them
Root port — all ports facing end devices are Root ports
Alternate port — end devices don't participate in STP
They vary depending on the host's STP priority
questions.explanation: STP roles are from the switch's perspective. Access ports connecting to end hosts (not other switches) have no competing bridge sending BPDUs. They become Designated ports (forwarding). Hosts send no BPDUs.
hints: Access ports to PCs = Designated (no competition, always forwarding)

questions.question: What happens when `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` is configured globally on a Cisco switch?
options: BPDU Guard is automatically enabled on all PortFast-enabled ports across the entire switch
BPDU Guard is enabled on all ports including trunk ports
Only manually configured BPDU Guard ports are affected
The command enables PortFast on all ports
questions.explanation: `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` enables BPDU Guard on every interface where PortFast is active. This is a best-practice global configuration to protect all access ports.
hints: bpduguard default = BPDU Guard on all PortFast ports globally

questions.question: Two switches are directly connected with a cable but `show cdp neighbors` shows no neighbor. What is the most likely cause?
options: CDP is disabled on one or both switches (or on the connecting interface)
The link is a trunk port
The switches are from different vendors
The switches are in different VLANs
questions.explanation: CDP must be enabled globally (`cdp run`) AND on the interface (`no cdp disable` or check `cdp enable`). If CDP is disabled on either switch or either interface, no neighbor is shown.
hints: No CDP neighbor: check 'show cdp' (global) and 'show cdp interface' (per-port)

questions.question: Which trunking mode will form a trunk only if the neighbor requests it, but won't initiate trunk negotiation itself?
questions.explanation: DTP modes: access=never trunk, dynamic auto=form trunk only if neighbor initiates (passive), dynamic desirable=actively try to form trunk, trunk=always trunk regardless.
hints: dynamic auto = passive (won't initiate); dynamic desirable = active (initiates)

questions.question: What does `show interfaces trunk` show in the 'VLANs in spanning tree forwarding state and not pruned' column?
options: The VLANs that are actually forwarding traffic on this trunk — in the allowed list, active in VLAN DB, and STP is in forwarding state
All VLANs in the allowed list
All active VLANs on the switch
questions.explanation: The final column shows VLANs that meet all three conditions: in the allowed list, exist in the VLAN database, AND are in STP forwarding state. This is the definitive list of VLANs actually passing traffic.
hints: show interfaces trunk final column = VLANs actually forwarding (all three conditions met)

questions.question: Which VLAN range is classified as 'extended VLAN' in Cisco's VLAN classification?
questions.explanation: Cisco VLAN ranges: Normal (1–1005), Extended (1006–4094). VLANs 1002-1005 are reserved for legacy protocols. Extended VLANs require VTP transparent mode or VTP v3.
hints: Normal VLANs: 1-1005; Extended: 1006-4094; VTP v1/v2 only supports normal range

questions.question: What is the purpose of a wireless probe request?
options: A client broadcasts Probe Requests to discover available SSIDs; APs respond with Probe Responses containing SSID and capabilities
Probe requests are sent by APs to measure interference
Probes are used to test WPA2 authentication
Probe frames carry payload data to test link quality
questions.explanation: Active scanning: client sends Probe Request to all channels. APs that receive it respond with Probe Response (SSID, supported rates, security). Allows faster network discovery than passive Beacon listening.
hints: Probe Request = active scanning by client; AP responds with Probe Response

questions.scenario_text: A network engineer is configuring a Cisco Catalyst switch. PC-A is assigned to VLAN 10 (Finance) and PC-B is in VLAN 20 (HR). Both PCs connect to the same physical switch but must be isolated from each other at Layer 2.

sub_questions.question: Without a Layer 3 routing device, which statement about VLAN 10 and VLAN 20 is correct?
options: They can communicate through the switch's backplane
They cannot communicate — VLANs are separate broadcast domains at Layer 2
They can communicate using static routes on the switch
They can communicate if both ports are in STP forwarding state
sub_questions.explanation: VLANs segment a switch into separate Layer 2 broadcast domains. Without a router or Layer 3 switch to route between VLANs, inter-VLAN communication is impossible.

sub_questions.question: A port connecting an end device (PC) to VLAN 10 should be configured as which port type?
sub_questions.explanation: Access ports carry traffic for a single VLAN. End devices connect to access ports. Trunk ports carry tagged traffic for multiple VLANs between switches and routers.

sub_questions.question: The native VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk carries frames without a VLAN tag.
sub_questions.explanation: 802.1Q defines a native VLAN whose frames are sent untagged across a trunk. Both ends must agree on the native VLAN to avoid frames appearing in the wrong VLAN.

questions.scenario_text: Three switches (SW1, SW2, SW3) are connected in a triangle topology. SW1 has a bridge priority of 4096, SW2 and SW3 have default priorities (32768). All MACs differ. STP is running to prevent a Layer 2 loop.

sub_questions.question: Which switch becomes the root bridge?
options: SW1 — it has the lowest bridge priority
SW2 — it has a higher MAC address than SW3
SW3 — it uses the default priority
All three — they share the root role
sub_questions.explanation: The root bridge is elected by lowest bridge ID. Bridge ID = priority + MAC. SW1's priority (4096) is lower than the default (32768), so SW1 wins regardless of MAC.

sub_questions.question: On non-root switches, which STP port role sends/receives traffic toward the root bridge via the best path?
sub_questions.explanation: Each non-root switch has exactly one Root Port — the port with the lowest cost (best path) to the root bridge.

sub_questions.question: In Rapid PVST+ (802.1w), an edge port (PortFast) transitions directly to forwarding state without waiting 30 seconds.
sub_questions.explanation: PortFast / edge ports skip the Listening and Learning STP states, going directly to forwarding. This is safe only on ports connected to end devices (not other switches).

questions.scenario_text: Two Cisco switches (SW-Core and SW-Dist) are connected with four parallel Gigabit Ethernet links. The team wants to bundle them into a single logical channel to increase throughput and provide link redundancy without STP blocking any links.

sub_questions.question: Which EtherChannel negotiation protocol is standards-based and works between Cisco and non-Cisco switches?
sub_questions.explanation: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) is the standards-based protocol for EtherChannel negotiation. PAgP is Cisco-proprietary.

sub_questions.question: If all four 1-Gbps links are active in the Port-Channel, what is the maximum aggregate bandwidth?
options: 1 Gbps — STP limits to one path
sub_questions.explanation: EtherChannel aggregates physical link bandwidth. Four 1-Gbps links = 4 Gbps aggregate (each direction). Load balancing distributes flows across all active links.

sub_questions.question: The channel-group command is applied to individual physical interfaces, not to the Port-channel interface directly.
sub_questions.explanation: You configure 'channel-group N mode active/passive/on' on each physical member interface. The Port-channel interface is created automatically and inherits the configuration.

questions.scenario_text: A company is deploying 50 lightweight APs across three office floors. A Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) sits in the data centre. The APs must be centrally managed and all client traffic must flow through the WLC for policy enforcement.

sub_questions.question: Which protocol do lightweight APs use to form a tunnel to the WLC?
sub_questions.explanation: CAPWAP (Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points) is the current standard. It uses UDP port 5246 (control) and 5247 (data) to tunnel AP-controller communications.

sub_questions.question: In a split-MAC (centralised) CAPWAP deployment, where are 802.11 management frames (association, authentication) processed?
sub_questions.explanation: In split-MAC architecture, the WLC handles 802.11 management (association, authentication, security) while the AP handles real-time 802.11 frame transmission/reception.

sub_questions.question: A FlexConnect AP can continue to bridge client traffic locally to the wired network even if the CAPWAP tunnel to the WLC fails.
sub_questions.explanation: FlexConnect (formerly HREAP) allows APs to forward data locally when the WLC is unreachable. This is useful for branch offices with limited WAN bandwidth or WLC availability.

questions.scenario_text: An engineer configures inter-VLAN routing using Router-on-a-Stick. The physical interface G0/0 on the router connects to a trunk port on the switch. VLANs 10, 20, and 99 (native) are in use. The router must route between VLAN 10 and VLAN 20.

sub_questions.question: Which sub-interface command tags frames for VLAN 10 on subinterface G0/0.10?
sub_questions.explanation: 'encapsulation dot1q 10' configures the subinterface to tag outgoing frames with VLAN 10 and accept only VLAN 10-tagged frames inbound.

sub_questions.question: What happens to frames tagged with VLAN 99 (the native VLAN) as they cross the 802.1Q trunk?
sub_questions.explanation: The native VLAN is sent untagged on an 802.1Q trunk. The receiving side assumes untagged frames belong to the native VLAN. Mismatch causes frames to appear in the wrong VLAN.

sub_questions.question: The native VLAN must be identical on both ends of an 802.1Q trunk link to prevent traffic from appearing in the wrong VLAN.
sub_questions.explanation: If native VLANs differ (e.g., one end uses VLAN 1, the other VLAN 99), untagged frames are sent into one VLAN but received into another — a VLAN hopping / mismatch vulnerability.

questions.question: Which wireless security protocol is considered most secure for enterprise networks?
questions.explanation: WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X with a RADIUS server for per-user authentication. WPA2-Personal uses a shared pre-shared key (PSK) — if compromised, all devices are vulnerable. WEP and WPA are deprecated.

questions.question: What is a rogue access point?
options: An AP configured with the wrong SSID
An unauthorized AP connected to the corporate network that may allow unauthorized access
An AP that only broadcasts on 5GHz
questions.explanation: A rogue AP is an unauthorized AP connected to the corporate wired network. It may be set up by an employee for convenience or by an attacker. Detected using wireless intrusion detection systems (WIDS) or WLC scanning.

questions.question: An attacker creates an AP with the same SSID as the corporate network to intercept traffic. This is called:
questions.explanation: An Evil Twin is a rogue AP that mimics a legitimate SSID to lure clients. Clients connect thinking it's legitimate; attacker performs MitM. Different from a passive rogue AP — this is actively adversarial.

questions.question: In STP, which bridge wins the root bridge election?
options: The bridge with the highest Bridge ID
The bridge with the lowest Bridge ID
The bridge with the highest IP address
The bridge with the most active ports
questions.explanation: STP elects the bridge with the lowest Bridge ID (BID) as root. BID = priority (default 32768) + VLAN ID + MAC address. Lowest MAC breaks ties when priority is equal.
distractor_notes: Highest BID loses the election — STP always prefers the lowest value, following the 'lowest wins' convention throughout STP.
IP addresses are a Layer 3 concept — STP operates entirely at Layer 2 and has no awareness of IP.
Port count is not part of STP's election algorithm — a switch with fewer ports can still win if its BID is lowest.

questions.question: How does an 802.1Q trunk port handle frames belonging to the native VLAN?
options: It drops them to prevent native VLAN leakage
It tags them with the native VLAN ID
questions.explanation: Native VLAN frames are sent untagged across an 802.1Q trunk. Both ends must agree on the native VLAN ID or frames will be misassigned.
distractor_notes: VLAN 0 is reserved — native VLAN frames carry no tag at all, not a VLAN 0 tag.
Dropping native VLAN frames would break the trunk — native VLAN forwarding untagged is standard 802.1Q behaviour.
Native VLAN frames are specifically NOT tagged — being sent untagged is the defining characteristic of the native VLAN.

questions.question: Which LACP mode combination successfully forms an EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: LACP active mode sends LACP PDUs. active–active forms a bundle because both sides initiate. passive–passive fails because neither side initiates.
distractor_notes: passive–passive means both sides wait for the other to initiate — neither sends LACP PDUs, so no EtherChannel forms.
'on' forces the channel without negotiation; combining it with LACP active causes a protocol mismatch and no bundle forms.
'desirable' is a PAgP keyword, not LACP — mixing PAgP and LACP modes on the two ends prevents EtherChannel formation.

questions.question: Which statement about a switch access port is correct?
options: It carries traffic for multiple VLANs using 802.1Q tags
It belongs to exactly one VLAN and does not tag frames
It must have a native VLAN configured
It requires DTP to be enabled
questions.explanation: An access port is assigned to a single VLAN. Frames entering and leaving an access port are untagged — the VLAN association is maintained internally by the switch.
distractor_notes: Carrying multiple VLANs with 802.1Q tags describes a trunk port, not an access port.
Native VLANs are a trunk port concept — access ports belong to one VLAN and have no native VLAN setting.
DTP negotiates trunk formation — it is not required for access ports, and best practice is to disable it on them.

questions.question: Which STP port state listens to BPDUs but does NOT learn MAC addresses or forward frames?
questions.explanation: Listening receives/sends BPDUs and participates in STP election, but neither learns MACs nor forwards data.

source_ref.text: Jeremy's IT Lab — Spanning Tree Protocol

questions.question: When a switch receives a frame for an unknown MAC address, what does it do?
options: Floods the frame out all ports except the incoming port
Forwards it to the default gateway
questions.explanation: Unknown unicast flooding — the switch floods the frame out all ports except the ingress port.
